parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with symmetric endowments (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN) but have different private values for RED and BLUE. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. RED is worth $0.60 to buffalo, $1.00 to cat and duck. BLUE is worth $0.10 to buffalo, $1.00 to cat, and $0.70 to duck. So BLUE is almost worthless to buffalo but quite valuable to the others, and RED is especially valuable to cat and duck. This creates clear gains from trade: buffalo wants to dump BLUE and possibly GREEN in exchange for RED; cat wants both RED and BLUE but can give up some GREEN; duck wants RED but is more balanced on BLUE. However, each player only knows their own values, so they have to infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and acceptances.

Strategically, buffalo has low acceptance thresholds, high bookkeeping discipline, and low aggressiveness, so she is likely to propose and accept deals that give her modest but clear positive surplus, especially trades that convert BLUE into RED or GREEN. She is comfortable with unequal splits and prefers closure, so she may accept even mildly disadvantageous-looking terms if they are simple and certain. Cat is more patient, with medium exchange aggressiveness and moderate acceptance thresholds; she values both RED and BLUE at $1, so from her perspective RED and BLUE are interchangeable and both far better than GREEN. She will look to trade away GREEN for either color and be more selective in accepting trades that give up much RED or BLUE. Duck has very low acceptance thresholds and high numeracy; he is willing to accept many positive-surplus trades, even if they look unequal, and can calculate payoffs precisely. He values RED at $1 and BLUE at $0.70, so he will eagerly buy RED with GREEN or even BLUE if cheap.

The main uncertainty comes from not knowing others’ private values or how tough they are. Early in the game, players will test simple ratios (e.g., 1 RED for 2 BLUE or 2 GREEN) to probe others’ willingness to trade. Since there are only three rounds and each player gets only three proposal turns, there is limited time to explore. This pushes toward relatively straightforward, positive-surplus deals rather than complex experimentation. Multi-acceptance randomness also matters: if both potential recipients like a deal, the proposer cannot choose the partner, so they may slightly favor offers that are acceptable to at least one target but not too attractive to both. Over the three rounds, we should see buffalo steadily convert BLUE (and some GREEN) into RED, cat trade away GREEN for RED or BLUE where possible, and duck opportunistically accept most positive-surplus opportunities while occasionally offering trades that cheaply acquire RED from the others.

Below is a plausible full trajectory for the three-round game, assuming reasonably rational but myopic play and using the personas as priors for selectivity and bargaining style.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one knows others’ valuations, so the focus is on simple, exploratory trades that almost surely benefit the proposer. Buffalo, with low aggressiveness and strong bookkeeping, is likely to start with a cautiously favorable offer that improves her mix (fewer BLUE, more RED or GREEN) but looks at least somewhat acceptable to the others. Cat will then respond by trying to swap low-value GREEN for high-value RED or BLUE, testing how much the others value GREEN. Duck, being numerate and a deal-ready accepter, will mostly look for clean opportunities to pick up RED or BLUE cheaply, but may also try one exploratory proposal to see whether others will part with RED if compensated with GREEN or BLUE.

By the second round, a few signals are in place. Buffalo’s first trade (selling many BLUE to get RED) tells the others that BLUE may be relatively low value to her and RED relatively high. Cat’s failed attempt to buy BLUE with GREEN suggests she values BLUE more than GREEN but misjudged counterparts’ willingness to give up BLUE. Duck’s declined offer to buy RED with GREEN shows the others are hesitant to surrender RED for GREEN at the tested rate. With this information, buffalo will continue to trade away BLUE, now more aggressively targeting cat. Cat will refine her attempt to unload GREEN in exchange for RED or BLUE, and duck, seeing reluctance to sell RED cheaply, may try to exploit buffalo’s apparent dislike of BLUE by offering RED for a large bundle of BLUE or GREEN.

Going into the final round, some regularities are clearer. Buffalo has shown consistent willingness to give up BLUE to obtain RED from both partners, and to accept BLUE in exchange for RED when the ratio strongly favors her. Cat appears reluctant to part with GREEN at ratios others like and has twice failed to get BLUE for GREEN, suggesting she misreads others’ valuation of GREEN. Duck has managed to buy a bit of RED with BLUE from buffalo but failed to buy RED with GREEN, revealing that GREEN is a weak currency for purchasing RED. With only one proposal turn left each, everyone has an incentive to secure any remaining easy gains. Buffalo will likely attempt one last BLUE-for-RED or BLUE-for-GREEN exchange that is clearly favorable to the counterparty. Cat, realizing GREEN is hard to spend, may make a more generous final offer to turn GREEN into either RED or BLUE. Duck, very tolerant and numerate, will target a final high-return trade, probably buying RED with a mix he suspects the seller undervalues, or selling a slightly overpriced bundle of BLUE or GREEN for RED.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player game has all players starting with 10 of each chip color and common knowledge that green is always $0.50, while red and blue values differ privately. Butterfly and Squid both value red and blue highly and fairly similarly (red is best, blue close behind), while Kangaroo values blue very little and red moderately. This creates strong gains-from-trade opportunities where Kangaroo sells blue (low value to her) for red or green, and where Butterfly or Squid sell some blue or green to upgrade into red. Because players don’t see others’ values, they must infer who values which color from what offers and acceptances appear. With three rounds and each player getting three proposal turns, there is enough time to test the market, but not so much that they can wait forever; proposals will likely start somewhat exploratory and become more targeted once someone’s preferences are partially revealed. Butterfly is patient, disciplined, and has a very low acceptance threshold, so she is willing to accept modest but positive gains and will often propose reasonably balanced trades that are clearly good for her but might also be acceptable to others. Kangaroo is more aggressive and exploratory with low acceptance thresholds: she wants to turn low-value blue into either green (safe) or red (moderate) and is willing to test several structures, including steeply favorable terms when she can. Squid is numerate, patient, and somewhat cooperative, with medium acceptance thresholds and some sensitivity to anchors: she won’t jump at tiny gains but is open to mutually beneficial terms and will pay attention to the exchange ratios implied by early offers. The main uncertainty is which chip type each player appears to be chasing; once someone reveals a strong preference, others can adjust. Multi-acceptance risk matters: since the recipient is drawn randomly if both non-senders accept, proposers must design offers that are good regardless of which counterpart they get. Overall, we should see repeated attempts by Kangaroo to dump blue, while Butterfly and Squid jockey to convert green and blue into red at favorable rates, with moderate but not extreme haggling around exchange ratios of roughly 1 red ≈ 1–1.2 blue or ≈1–1.5 green from each proposer’s private perspective.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, no one has information about others’ chip values beyond the common green value. Players are likely to make relatively simple, one-color-for-one-color trades that clearly benefit themselves, probing who bites. Butterfly moves first and will probably test turning some blue or green into red on generous but self-favorable terms, expecting at least one counterpart to accept small positive gains. Kangaroo then has a chance to start unloading low-value blue or to grab red if she suspects someone values it less. Squid, acting last, can update from observed acceptances and refusals and may respond with more calibrated proposals that target whichever chip colors seem cheap in the emerging “market.”

By round 1, some structure is revealed. Butterfly has traded green for red with Kangaroo, implying Butterfly seems to like red more than green and Kangaroo is at least willing to add red at a mild cost. Kangaroo then traded away blue for green to Squid, signaling that blue is relatively cheap for her and that Squid is happy to take blue when the rate is favorable. No one accepted Squid’s blue-for-red offer at parity, suggesting that both Butterfly and Kangaroo place a relatively high value on red compared to blue. With these signals, Butterfly’s second proposal will likely refine toward swapping away whichever chip she now values least (probably green or possibly blue) for red at a rate that still looks decent to someone. Kangaroo will keep trying to monetize her remaining blue or adjust her portfolio toward more green/red. Squid, after a declined offer, will update and may propose slightly more generous terms or target Kangaroo as the more promising blue seller or green holder.

In the final round, players know this is their last chance to improve portfolios, so they are more willing to accept even modest gains. Butterfly has already upgraded a mix of green and blue into red and is close to her preferred composition; she will only propose trades with clear positive value and may accept small, safe improvements. Kangaroo has aggressively rebalanced toward green and red and still holds some blue she would like to shed if the rate is fair. Squid has collected extra blue and some additional red, but still values further red upgrades and will be attentive to any last-chance swap that turns green or blue into red. Proposals now tend to be simpler and closer to “fair” ratios from each proposer’s perspective, with slightly more generous terms to increase the odds of getting a deal through before the game ends.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips ($0.50 for everyone) and privately valued red and blue chips. All three players start with symmetric inventories (10 of each color) but asymmetric values: bat values red and blue at $0.80; fox values blue at $0.90 and red at $0.40; frog values blue at $1.00 and red at $0.50. All have very low rejection thresholds and low exploitation caution, so they are inclined to accept any trade that yields them a small positive gain, even if the proposer benefits more. Because each player sees only their own values, they must infer others’ valuations from what colors they try to buy cheaply and what they are willing to sell. The key surplus comes from moving red chips from fox (who values them least) toward bat, and blue chips from bat (who values them least among the three) toward frog and fox. However, each trade is bilateral and quantity-limited by current holdings, and recipients are chosen randomly when multiple players accept, so proposers must design offers that are self-profitable against any possible recipient. Given the players’ deal-ready, low-search profiles, we expect relatively simple, moderately generous offers rather than highly optimized sequences, with quick acceptance of any clearly positive opportunity. Over three rounds (nine proposals), early trades should reveal rough color preferences, with later proposals slightly adapting to observed behavior but still remaining myopic and inventory-aware rather than fully strategic or collusive.

rounds.round_explanation: At the outset, everyone holds 10 of each color and knows only their own values. Given their prosocial, low-threshold profiles, early offers will likely be modest and exploratory, testing whether others value particular colors. Bat is numerate and may open with a mildly favorable but not extreme exchange. Fox, valuing red relatively low, is inclined to sell red for blue or possibly green at slightly generous rates. Because nobody is strongly guarded, the first genuinely positive offers for each recipient are likely to be accepted without prolonged search, and small early trades will start revealing color preferences through who accepts what.

After round 0, inventories have shifted: bat remains at 10 of each color, having traded nothing. Fox has gained blue and lost some red and green; frog has gained blue and lost red. The pattern already suggests that fox and frog are keen to accumulate blue and are relatively willing to shed red or green. Bat can infer that blue is attractive to others, and that fox is at least somewhat indifferent to red. With this limited evidence, bat is now better positioned to act as a blue seller and red buyer against fox and frog. Fox, observing frog’s enthusiasm for buying blue with red, may continue to offload red for blue. Frog, seeing that both others will pay for blue, might pivot to acquiring more blue using their relatively less valuable red or green, but must pay attention to their now-reduced red stock. All three still have low acceptance thresholds, so they are prepared to take moderate but safe surplus moves even if they are not perfectly balanced.

By the final round, the trading pattern is clearer. Fox and frog have repeatedly accepted deals that give them blue, and fox has been comfortable selling red; frog has been comfortable selling red and green if it leads to more blue. Bat has now executed at least one profitable red-for-blue swap, revealing that bat is a willing counterparty for red and not especially attached to blue. None of the players appear punitive or strategic in a long-horizon sense, and with only one round left, strategic patience is less relevant. Instead, each proposer focuses on any remaining straightforward surplus, especially moving red from fox toward bat and blue toward fox and frog if the proposer benefits. Offers remain simple 1:1 or small-ratio trades, just good enough that low-threshold recipients will accept, with more emphasis on clearing obviously dominated holdings like fox’s low-valued red in exchange for either green or especially blue.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players have symmetric portfolios and no information about others’ private values beyond public ranges. The first wave of proposals is likely to be exploratory, testing whether others are willing to trade BLUE or RED for GREEN at roughly even or slightly skewed rates. Given dog-green-8079’s low aggressiveness and high acceptance flexibility, he is likely to propose a deal that modestly improves his own payoff while still obviously attractive to someone who values BLUE more. Entering the final round, players know the game is about to end, so strategic patience matters less than immediate surplus. All three players value GREEN chips equally at $0.50, so GREEN acts as a stable numeraire and is unlikely to be traded at a loss. Private values differ mainly on BLUE chips: owl values BLUE highest ($0.70), mouse at $0.40, dog at $0.30. RED is worth $0.50 to dog and owl, but only $0.40 to mouse. This means efficient trade patterns give more BLUE to owl (from dog/mouse) and more RED to dog/owl (from mouse) when exchange rates produce mutual dollar gains. However, each player only knows their own values; they must infer others' preferences from observed deals within a very short 3-round horizon.

Given all three personas have relatively low acceptance thresholds and are described as deal-ready, they are inclined to accept any strictly positive surplus offers, even if somewhat unequal. Mouse and owl are somewhat more numerate and exploratory; owl, with the strongest bookkeeping, is best positioned to identify that BLUE is especially valuable to him and to pay small premiums in RED or GREEN to acquire BLUE from dog and mouse.

Uncertainty centers on two things: (1) whether others value BLUE much more or much less than oneself, and (2) whether any player is attempting to exploit this with steep price ratios. With only three rounds (nine proposal turns total) and one-sided UI calculators (each sees only their own surplus), players will mostly stick to simple, clearly profitable trades rather than complex multi-step strategies. We therefore forecast a game with several accepted trades that modestly reallocate BLUE toward owl and RED away from mouse, with GREEN used sparingly as a payment chip at roughly par value.

The main friction is the random recipient rule when multiple acceptances occur: proposers cannot choose who they trade with, so they may craft offers that are attractive to at least one counterpart but not obviously generous to both, to avoid heavy multi-acceptance uncertainty. However, because all three are relatively tolerant and not strongly suspicious, this friction will manifest mainly as occasional randomization of trade partners rather than outright breakdown of bargaining. As their own turns come, mouse and owl will mimic this pattern with straightforward proposals that try to gain a bit in dollar terms while exploring who is more eager to buy or sell BLUE.

By Round 1, some information has been revealed. Dog has shown willingness to trade GREEN for BLUE at roughly equal personal value, and mouse has accepted BLUE-for-GREEN at a small gain, suggesting to observant players that mouse does not value BLUE especially highly. Owl has successfully bought BLUE from dog with a small premium in GREEN, confirming that owl values BLUE relatively more. Owl now has both more BLUE and less GREEN than at the start, while dog has traded away some BLUE and GREEN but gained surplus. Players update their beliefs: dog may infer owl is a strong demander of BLUE, and mouse may infer that both others are happy with BLUE/RED-GREEN swaps near parity.

Strategically, this round should feature more targeted attempts to move BLUE from mouse to owl or dog, and to move RED from mouse to dog or owl, but without extreme price ratios. Dog will likely continue making simple, closure-oriented offers that keep her surplus non-negative and might slightly favor owl if she interprets owl's prior acceptance as eagerness. Mouse, seeing his initial proposal declined, may adjust towards more attractive terms, especially in BLUE-for-RED trades. Owl, with strong bookkeeping, will now deliberately seek BLUE from mouse at favorable rates, giving up some RED or remaining GREEN to secure additional high-value BLUE.

Entering the final round, all players see a history dominated by BLUE-GREEN and BLUE-RED trades where BLUE tends to flow toward owl and, to a lesser extent, dog, while mouse often parts with BLUE when compensated modestly in RED or GREEN. Everyone now has a clearer picture: owl is the highest BLUE valuer, mouse is relatively BLUE-indifferent compared with RED/GREEN, and dog sits in the middle but is highly willing to accept positive-sum trades. With only three proposal turns left, strategic patience is low in practice: players are inclined to lock in any remaining easy gains.

Dog is likely to make a final, moderate BLUE-for-RED or BLUE-for-GREEN trade that yields a small but safe surplus, targeting mouse as the more probable accepter. Mouse, with prior rejections behind him, will calibrate to offer clearly positive terms to at least one counterpart, perhaps giving BLUE to owl or dog at nearly even or slightly favorable rates. Owl’s last move is to top up his BLUE stock where possible, paying with RED or GREEN, but without offering overly generous rates given the short horizon. The result is a final smoothing of BLUE and RED holdings toward efficiency but not a complete equalization, constrained by limited remaining chips and the small number of turns.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players share the same value for GREEN ($0.50), but value RED and BLUE slightly differently. Alligator and Buffalo have identical values (RED $1.00, BLUE $0.70), while Rabbit values both a bit less (RED $0.90, BLUE $0.60). This makes Alligator and Buffalo natural high-value buyers of RED and BLUE compared with Rabbit, but the cross-player value differences are quite small. Given RED and BLUE are known only within a broad public range ($0.10–$1.00), everyone knows that gains from trade exist if someone values a color more than someone else does, but they do not know exactly who has which valuations. With common-value GREEN at $0.50, GREEN serves mostly as a neutral medium or filler in trades rather than a strong target of speculation.

Personality-wise, Alligator and Rabbit are both deal-ready with very low acceptance thresholds and low exploitation caution; they are likely to accept any offer that gives them even a small positive surplus. Both have high bookkeeping discipline, so they are unlikely to make obvious arithmetic mistakes and will notice even small gains. Buffalo is more prosocial and less aggressive, moderately patient, somewhat anchor-sensitive, and has mixed acceptance thresholds. She will not systematically insist on tough terms and is willing to accept reasonable but not tiny-gain offers, especially when framed as fair or balanced.

The key uncertainties are (1) who values which color more, (2) how strict others’ acceptance thresholds are, and (3) how much partner competition matters when two responders can both accept. Because responders accept privately and a random accepting recipient is chosen, proposers cannot target a specific partner and must design offers that are attractive to at least one counterpart while avoiding giving away too much if both accept. Given the small valuation differences and short 3-round horizon, players are unlikely to engineer complex multi-step plans; instead, they will test simple, roughly balanced-looking offers early and then, if those are accepted, possibly repeat or slightly tweak them.

The most likely overall pattern is that at least one mutually beneficial RED↔BLUE or RED/BLUE↔GREEN trade occurs, with Alligator and Buffalo often in the role of buyers of RED or BLUE at favorable rates. Rabbit—being more prosocial and slightly lower-valuing—will often be the one who agrees to trade away some higher-value chips from Alligator/Buffalo’s perspective in exchange for what looks like a fair quantity on her screen. Because all three are relatively accepting and two of them are quite unguarded, the game is more likely to feature accepted trades than a complete impasse, but not all proposals will be accepted: some initial, more exploratory offers will be too skewed or confusing to clear both responders’ thresholds, especially when Buffalo is on the responding side and feels the offer is too lopsided.

In this reconstructed game, early rounds feature exploratory but modestly self-favoring offers. Players quickly see which rates are acceptable to at least one counterpart and then recycle successful structures. Alligator and Rabbit are willing to lock in small positive gains, while Buffalo sometimes passes on marginal deals but is happy to accept ones that look reasonably balanced and reduce uncertainty about ending with unused chips.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, everyone holds 10 of each chip. No one yet knows the others’ exact valuations, so the first few offers are exploratory. With three full rounds ahead, players are willing to test terms but not so patient that they avoid all early trade. Alligator, as a disciplined but low-threshold accepter, is likely to propose a small but clearly self-profitable trade that still appears roughly fair. Buffalo, being prosocial and somewhat anchor-sensitive, will tend to mirror fairness-looking ratios. Rabbit, with very low acceptance threshold and high numeracy, is ready to accept small gains and will propose modestly self-favoring but clearly win–win terms, especially when the UI shows a green (positive) surplus for her side.

By Round 1, two trades have executed, both with Rabbit as the accepting partner. Alligator has slightly more BLUE and fewer GREEN; Buffalo has slightly more RED and fewer GREEN; Rabbit has given up some BLUE and RED and gained GREEN, which is neutral in value for everyone. Publicly, the others see Rabbit as willing to accept balanced-looking GREEN-for-color trades, hinting that Rabbit’s valuations may not be dramatically different. Now, proposers adjust: Alligator and Buffalo know Rabbit is easy to deal with, while Rabbit realizes she may have moved chips in ways that somewhat disadvantage her privately. Alligator will likely target Rabbit again with a mild but positive-surplus offer. Buffalo, now slightly richer in RED, may slow down on acquiring more RED and test a BLUE-related trade. Rabbit, after a declined offer last round, should refine her proposals toward more clearly win–win structures that visibly give the responder some surplus on their calculator.

Going into the final round, everyone has observed that Rabbit frequently accepts offers that look balanced in chip counts, especially GREEN-for-color trades, and that Buffalo is also willing to accept such terms when she is short on a color. Alligator has successfully improved his mix of higher-valued RED and BLUE relative to GREEN. Buffalo has cycled some RED and GREEN, and Rabbit has partially corrected earlier moves by reacquiring RED from Buffalo. With only one proposal left per player, there is less incentive to hold out for perfect terms; instead, each proposer looks for a last incremental gain that their preferred counterpart is likely to accept. Alligator, aware of Rabbit’s responsiveness, tries one more modest trade targeting her. Buffalo, more prosocial and aiming for closure, proposes a gentle, almost even exchange. Rabbit, now more aware of how her earlier trades affected her holdings, aims for a final offer that gives her a small but clear surplus while still being easy for at least one responder to accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player, three-round bargaining game features private values for red and blue chips and common value for green. Each player starts with 10 of each chip, and all can see the trade history and current inventories but only know their own dollar valuations. Goose values red highly (0.90) and blue at 0.50; bear values all three colors equally at 0.50; buffalo values red at 0.80, green at 0.50, and blue relatively poorly at 0.20. Because green is common-value at 0.50, it functions like a cash-like numeraire: everyone is indifferent between swapping green at equal quantities and will tend to trade greens mainly as a vehicle to move private-value chips. Red is particularly valuable to goose and buffalo, while bear is red-indifferent relative to other colors; blue is especially weak for buffalo. Efficient trades tend to move red toward goose and buffalo and move blue away from buffalo toward others, with green often used to compensate. However, strategic uncertainty is high: each player knows their own valuations and sees the UI’s surplus feedback, but they do not know exactly how the others value chips, only that values lie in the given public ranges. This leads to exploratory offers and some declined trades, especially early. Goose has low exchange aggressiveness and a low acceptance threshold; she is numerate, patient, and willing to accept modest but positive gains quickly, especially when she can cheaply give lower-value blue or green to gain high-value red. Bear is relatively patient, with mixed aggressiveness and moderate acceptance thresholds; since all chips are worth the same to her, she focuses on avoiding obvious losses and may be indifferent among many deals that slightly move her inventory, so she’ll accept only when someone offers her a clear surplus. Buffalo is numerate, state-aware, and also relatively easy to satisfy (low acceptance threshold), with a strong motive to convert low-value blue into higher-value red or at least neutral green. The random resolution when multiple partners accept gives proposers an incentive to structure offers to be acceptable to at least one counterpart while not leaving too much surplus on the table. Over three rounds, we expect some early feeling-out proposals, then more targeted attempts to move red toward goose and buffalo and blue away from buffalo. Given the short horizon (three rounds, each player proposes three times total) and deal-ready personas for goose and buffalo, several mutually beneficial trades are likely, mostly transferring red from bear (to whom it’s average) to goose and buffalo in exchange for green or blue, and transferring blue away from buffalo. The game we forecast below tracks one plausible, internally consistent path where players make reasonably calculated but non-omniscient proposals and respond based on their private net gains displayed by the surplus calculator.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players have symmetric inventories (10 of each chip) but asymmetric private values. No one yet has any direct evidence about the others’ valuations beyond the public ranges. Proposals this round are likely to be exploratory and anchored on simple patterns (e.g., trading 1-for-1 or 2-for-1 using green as a balancing chip). Goose, as the first proposer and a low-aggressiveness, low-threshold type, is inclined to offer a modestly advantageous but not extreme trade that favors her high-value red, hoping at least one partner will accept and reveal some information. Bear, valuing all colors equally, should be somewhat selective and patient, accepting only when a deal gives her extra chips or clearly positive surplus. Buffalo, with strong bookkeeping and a desire to offload low-value blue, will quickly spot opportunities to gain red or green in exchange for blue, but early uncertainty may make her cautious about trades that give away red. Random tie-breaking if multiple players accept is a secondary concern in this first round, where the main goal is testing the waters.

By the start of round 1, players have already observed three trades and updated inventories. Goose has increased her red stock by cheaply trading blue, signaling that at least one counterpart (bear) was willing to give up red for blue; bear has shown a willingness to trade red away both for blue and for green at near-value-neutral rates, hinting that she values colors similarly. Buffalo has just accepted red for green from bear, suggesting to observers that she may value red at least as much as green. Buffalo also tried converting blue into red with bear, revealing her own preference to move out of blue when possible. With this partial information, players now have more targeted expectations: goose and buffalo both see bear as a flexible red source, while bear recognizes that both counterparts like red and might be willing to overpay slightly for it. The second round thus shifts from exploratory to mildly exploitative bargaining: proposers begin to aim offers at the counterpart most likely to value the opposite side differently, while still staying within ranges they expect to be acceptable.

Heading into the final round, inventory imbalances are pronounced and informative. Goose has built up a strong red position by repeatedly buying red from bear with blue and green, indicating to everyone that red is particularly valuable to her. Bear, in turn, has been progressively shedding red in favor of blue and green, consistent with being color-indifferent and willing to supply red whenever she does not lose value. Buffalo has used bear as a counterparty to offload blue for red, but has fewer remaining blue chips and a healthier red stock than at the start. By round 2, each player understands the broad structure: goose and buffalo both like red more than blue, buffalo likes green more than blue, and bear is relatively neutral across colors. With only three proposal turns remaining, strategic patience gives way to a focus on seizing any remaining sure gains. Goose looks for one last chance to acquire red without giving up too much of her high-value red inventory; bear aims for small, safe improvements that don’t expose her to outright losses; and buffalo, deal-ready and numerate, will accept any clear upgrade from blue into red or at least into neutral green. However, inventories now constrain feasible trades: buffalo’s reduced blue stock limits further blue-for-red upgrades, and bear’s dwindling red supply caps how much more red can be moved. This final round therefore features marginal, opportunistic exchanges rather than large structural shifts.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a three-player chip-bargaining game with private values on red and blue chips and a common value on green chips. Each player starts with 10 chips of each color. Everyone knows that green is worth $0.50 to all players, but each player only knows their own red and blue values during play. From our analyst view: peacock values red highly ($0.80) and blue moderately ($0.50); cat values red and green equally ($0.50) and blue weakly ($0.30); otter values red ($0.70) much more than blue ($0.20). Surplus is created mainly by moving chips toward players who value them more (especially red toward peacock and otter, blue toward peacock and cat), and away from those who value them less (blue away from otter, red away from cat). However, players don't know others' exact values, so they tentatively explore exchange rates by proposing trades that are clearly profitable to themselves and plausibly acceptable to at least one partner. Because responses are simultaneous and the recipient is chosen at random among accepters, targeted offers to a specific counterpart are risky: a too-generous deal may be taken by the "wrong" partner and transfer surplus unintentionally. Empirically, all three personas are relatively low-threshold, deal-ready accepters with moderate patience, so we expect them to accept any strictly profitable trade and often to accept small-gain trades for simplicity, while still rejecting clear losses. Peacock is moderately exploratory and comfortable with unequal outcomes, likely to float several different structures but still likes closure and predictable gains. Cat is numerically disciplined, prosocial, and unguarded, so she will track chip counts carefully and accept many modest-win trades. Otter is less aggressive and quite anchor-sensitive, likely to be influenced by previous rates and to accept balanced or slightly favorable offers if they look fair. Across three short rounds (nine total proposal turns) we expect a few accepted trades that gradually reallocate red chips toward peacock and otter and blue chips toward peacock and cat, with small, fair-seeming ratios (often around 1:1 or slightly skewed) rather than extreme exploitation. Early moves are exploratory and conservative; later moves condition on observed acceptances and shifting inventories, but the finite horizon and shared preference for closure push players to lock in clearly profitable deals once found rather than wait for a perfect arrangement.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone holds 10 of each chip, and no one has seen any prior terms. Players only know their own values and the public ranges. They are likely to open with conservative, simple structures (1:1 or 1-for-2 involving green) that guarantee them a small gain at their own valuations and that seem plausibly acceptable to at least one other person under the public range beliefs. Because green is common-value and transparent, it is a natural "money" chip for early experimentation. Peacock, with relatively high red value, is incentivized to acquire red and give up blue or green; cat, with low blue value and decent red value, is happy to offload blue if she can gain red or green; otter strongly prefers red to blue. In this first round, players are exploring which directions of trade get bites and will update their expectations about counterpart preferences based on what is accepted.

By round 1, players have seen which general trade directions were accepted: cat accepted blue-for-red with peacock, otter accepted blue-for-green with cat, and peacock accepted red-for-green with otter. Publicly visible inventory shifts now hint at preferences: cat willingly traded away blue twice (indicating low blue value), otter gave up red in favor of green earlier (but actually values red highly, so this likely reflected perceived fair terms), and peacock both bought red and sold red at different prices, reflecting exploration around red–green and red–blue tradeoffs. With this limited history, players are still uncertain about exact valuations but now have some directional beliefs: red is broadly desired, blue is widely dumped, and green often acts as the bridging currency. Given their low acceptance thresholds and desire for closure, they are now more willing to repeat known-profitable patterns (cat selling blue, otter seeking red, peacock arbitraging between colors) while trying to tweak ratios slightly in their own favor. Inventory constraints also start to matter a bit, as earlier trades have changed who has relatively more of each chip.

Entering the final round, the players have a richer trading history and more informative inventories: blue has consistently flowed away from both cat and otter, red has tended to flow toward peacock and otter, and green has been the balancing chip. With only three proposal turns left, strategic patience diminishes and all three deal-ready personas are inclined to grab any clearly positive opportunity rather than hold out for an ideal but uncertain trade. Because red is now relatively concentrated and highly valued by both peacock and otter, proposals that move red will likely demand a premium in blue or green. Cat, having repeatedly offloaded blue and modestly increased red and green, can still profit by trading remaining blue for red or green at slightly favorable rates. Otter, having accumulated red and given up much blue, mainly benefits from minor rebalancing if she can cheaply acquire more green or offload residual low-value blue; peacock, with strong red and moderate blue values, may fine-tune holdings between red and green or between blue and green in a way that guarantees at least a small surplus. The last few trades are thus expected to be small, clean, and clearly surplus-enhancing for both sides, reflecting both learning from the earlier rounds and the shared preference for closing with a solid, if not maximized, payoff.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This 3-player, 3-round bargaining game centers on trading red and blue chips, which have different private values for each player, while green chips are common-value and symmetric. All begin with 10 of each color. Given the valuations, red is essentially worthless for Squid ($0.10) and moderately valuable for Camel ($0.40), while it is very valuable for Badger ($1.00). Blue is very valuable for both Squid and Badger ($1.00) and somewhat less so for Camel ($0.80). Green is worth $0.50 to everyone and so tends to be used sparingly because any green traded is a dollar-for-dollar cost to the sender and a dollar-for-dollar benefit to the receiver, making it hard to create large gains from trade with greens alone. The main efficiency opportunities come from moving red chips away from Squid (who values them much less) to Badger (who values them most) and Camel (who values them somewhat), financed by blue or possibly green chips in the other direction, and by potentially moving some blue chips away from Camel to Squid or Badger, since Camel values blue slightly less. However, because each proposer only sees their own values, they do not know exactly how attractive their offers are to others and must infer preferences from the ranges and from others’ accept/reject behavior. Squid and Camel both have low acceptance thresholds and low exploitation caution, so they are more willing to take small but positive gains, even if the deal is somewhat lopsided. Badger is more moderate and somewhat more guarded. Squid is numerically disciplined and likely to craft precise, self-beneficial trades that convert low-value red into higher-value blue or green with a margin that still leaves at least slight gains for plausible partners. Badger is moderately exploratory and will search for favorable rates like trading away green for high-value red, but will not routinely accept very thin or negative deals. Camel is a deal-ready accepter, tolerant of inequality, and disciplined enough to avoid obvious losses; Camel will accept many offers that yield even small positive gains. Dynamics across rounds are shaped by inventory changes: early trades that move red to Badger improve his position substantially, and after observing which trades are accepted, later proposers update their guesses about others’ valuations (for example, inferring that Badger likes red if he eagerly takes red-for-green or red-for-blue deals). Since simultaneous responses and random partner selection apply when multiple players accept, proposers are incentivized to craft offers that would be acceptable to more than one counterparty but may still be somewhat skewed in their favor. Across three rounds, we therefore expect a few accepted trades that gradually concentrate red with Badger and somewhat with Camel, and concentrate blue with Squid and Badger, with green used sparingly as a balancing or exploratory currency when direct red–blue trades seem risky. Some offers will be declined when they fail to give counterparties clear positive gains or when uncertainty about values makes them look unattractive, but the presence of two relatively easy accepters (Squid and Camel) makes at least one or two accepted trades per round plausible.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip color. None of them has yet observed acceptance behavior, so they rely on their own valuations, the public ranges, and intuitive expectations. The most obvious efficiency gains are from moving red away from Squid (who values red very little) to Badger (who values it highly) or Camel (who values it somewhat), and from moving some blue from Camel (who values it slightly less) toward Squid or Badger. Squid, with strong bookkeeping and a low acceptance threshold, is likely to open with an exploratory but mildly self-favoring trade that tests whether another player values blue or green enough to pay with red. Badger, moving second, has seen one data point (which parties accept or not) and will try to convert green (medium value) into red (high value) or possibly blue, while leaving obvious gains to encourage acceptance. Camel, with very low acceptance threshold and low exploitation caution, is willing to accept small but positive gains and may also propose modest, symmetric-looking trades that feel fair, particularly moving some red toward themselves if they suspect others undervalue it.

Going into Round 1, inventories have shifted due to two accepted trades in Round 0. Squid has traded away some low-value red to Camel in exchange for high-value blue, improving Squid’s portfolio of valuable chips. Camel now holds extra red and fewer blue and green, slightly increasing their exposure to a color they value moderately and reducing some medium- and lower-value chips. Badger has gained red from Camel in exchange for green, a very favorable move for Badger (since red is worth more than green to Badger) and only a modest sacrifice for Camel (green and red are closer in value for Camel). These outcomes provide informational cues: players can infer that Camel is willing to accept red-for-blue and red-for-green trades that offer modest surplus, and that Badger is happy to acquire red even at the cost of green. In this round, Squid is likely to continue converting remaining red into blue or green, perhaps now targeting Badger as a red buyer. Badger, having gained red, may pivot to seeking blue if that looks especially valuable relative to their holdings or may try to unload some additional green for more red if others still accept that. Camel, whose inventories now lean more red-heavy, may propose trades that rebalance back toward blue or green while still capturing some surplus, using their willingness to accept small gains to remain flexible.

By Round 2, repeated trades have progressively moved red away from Squid toward Badger and Camel, while shifting blue toward Squid and, to a lesser extent, Badger and Camel in response to earlier accepted offers. Green has been used sparingly, mainly as a payment vehicle when Badger or Camel perceived a chance to upgrade into higher-value red or blue. The accumulated history gives everyone better signals: Badger’s repeated interest in acquiring red and blue at reasonable prices reveals their high valuation for those colors; Camel’s willingness to accept trades with modest surplus shows a low acceptance threshold; Squid’s disciplined pattern of dumping red for blue or green suggests a consistently low red valuation. With only one round left, proposers have less incentive to hold back and more to lock in any remaining positive-sum trades, even if the surplus is small. Squid will likely try to make one last conversion of any remaining red into blue or green. Badger, seeing their portfolio already improved, may make a relatively aggressive final ask (e.g., trying to grab blue for green at favorable terms), some of which may now be declined as others recognize limited remaining opportunities. Camel, who has repeatedly accepted modestly beneficial trades, may use the final proposal to rebalance toward their preferred mix of blue and red with a fair-looking trade that is likely to draw at least one acceptance if it generates clear value for a counterpart.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features private values on red and blue chips, common value on green chips, and three structured rounds where each player proposes once per round. Everyone starts symmetric in quantities (10 of each color), but asymmetric in how much those chips are worth to them. Ram (ram-purple-9443) values red the most (0.90) and blue second (0.70); dog (dog-blue-6080) values both colored chips very low (red 0.20, blue 0.10); butterfly (butterfly-blue-3807) sits in the middle (red 0.70, blue 0.50). Green is worth 0.50 to all players. This means green is a safe, tradeable medium and also a natural reference: if a player can offload low-value red/blue in exchange for green, they increase surplus. Ram's best arbitrage is to acquire red (very valuable) while giving up green (average) or blue (also valuable but slightly less). Dog wants to turn almost worthless red/blue into green if possible, and will rarely want more red or blue unless the rate is very favorable. Butterfly is numerically disciplined and values red and blue moderately, so he can be a natural counterpart for both ram and dog: he may sell some green to ram or dog if he sees a clear gain and also trade red/blue between them at balanced or slightly favorable rates. All three personas share low acceptance thresholds and moderate-to-low exploitation caution, so they are inclined to accept even somewhat uneven deals that are still positive in their own payoff, especially early before inventories diverge too much. However, because responses are simultaneous and the realized recipient is random if multiple players accept, proposers must design offers that are at least weakly attractive to more than one counterpart or target one clear beneficiary while ensuring that any accidental alternative recipient would not be too costly. Over three rounds, as holdings start from symmetry and end after at most nine proposals, the likely pattern is: ram tests value-creating trades using green as payment to buy red; dog opportunistically sells low-value chips for green or for slightly higher-valued colors if discovered; butterfly, tracking totals carefully, accepts small positive-margin trades and may propose moderate, clearly win-win bundles that look obviously good to at least one counterpart. Uncertainty remains about what others value: players may infer relative valuations from what colors others try to buy or sell, but with only three rounds there is limited learning. The surplus calculator makes each accept/decline decision precise on one's own side, but not on others', so players mainly optimize myopic gains per trade with light strategic anticipation about who is likely to accept.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 red, 10 blue, and 10 green. No one has yet observed others' choices, so beliefs about others' private values are diffuse. Given their personas and values, ram is likely to begin by trying a small, safe test trade that slightly favors him if accepted. Dog will tend to offer something that converts her almost worthless colored chips into green, hoping at least one counterpart has higher value for those colors. Butterfly, with strong bookkeeping, will look for a clean, obviously positive trade and may be quick to lock in any small gain rather than waiting for more complex deals later. Because all acceptance thresholds are relatively low, modestly win-win offers that use green as payment stand a good chance of being accepted.

Going into round 1, several trades have already shifted holdings and revealed partial information. Ram has shown a willingness to pay green for red, signaling that red is relatively valuable to him. Dog has signaled that she is happy to dump red for green even at modest terms, implying low red value. Butterfly traded away some green for red and blue, which suggests moderate or higher values for those colors. With this emerging pattern, players can update beliefs: ram likely values red more than green, dog likely values green more than red, and butterfly is willing to arbitrage between them when he sees positive surplus. Now, proposals are more targeted. Ram will try to buy more red or perhaps blue from dog on favorable terms. Dog will continue to offload low-value colored chips, especially red, likely targeting ram who appears to like red. Butterfly will look to slightly rebalance if he sees profitable ways to reverse or extend earlier trades, mindful that his green stock has declined and he must avoid overpaying for marginal reds or blues.

By round 2, trade history has made patterns clear: dog is systematically moving red and blue out in exchange for green, ram is consistently acquiring red for green at favorable rates, and butterfly has been the numerically careful middleman trading some green for both red and blue when profitable. Inventories are now somewhat skewed: ram holds more red and somewhat fewer green than at the start; dog holds more green and fewer colored chips; butterfly has rebalanced multiple times and sits with a diversified but adjusted mix. With only one proposal left for each player, strategic patience gives way to final optimization. Ram may attempt a last small trade to convert surplus green into high-value red or blue if possible. Dog may try a final dump of low-value colored chips to gain a bit more green, provided she still holds some red or blue. Butterfly will be especially sensitive to marginal gains, declining any trade that would reduce his total value by even a little, and looking only for clearly positive, low-risk arbitrage. Given their low acceptance thresholds but finite time, most accepted trades now are simple, small-quantity exchanges aimed at squeezing out a few cents of surplus from remaining mismatches in valuations.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with 10 of each chip color. Green is common-value ($0.50) so its value is transparent: everyone knows green is relatively high compared with at least some of the plausible red/blue values. Red and blue are privately valued and allowed to vary between $0.10 and $1.00, so from each player's perspective, opponents might value red/blue anywhere in that range. Given the actual values, iguana and alligator both value blue highly ($0.70) and red moderately ($0.50/$0.30), while lion values both red and blue low ($0.20). This makes lion the natural seller of red/blue in exchange for green, and makes iguana/alligator natural buyers of red and especially blue if the perceived price is low enough.

Because everyone knows the public ranges but not the exact private values, each player will infer others' valuations only through observed acceptance behavior and trade patterns. The UI surplus calculator means no one will accept a trade that they know is strictly worse in dollar terms, but uncertainty about others' values means proposers must experiment and shade offers toward their own advantage. Persona cues indicate that all players have relatively low acceptance thresholds and low exploitation caution, with lion especially patient and exploratory, and iguana/alligator reasonably willing to cut deals. That makes accepted trades likely in early rounds, and rebalancing over multiple turns likely as players update beliefs about who values which color.

A central dynamic is that lion should try to offload low-value red/blue for green, potentially offering slightly generous terms at first to discover who bites. Iguana should try to acquire red (and maybe blue) at favorable rates using green or possibly over-supplied color, but must watch inventory to preserve enough green for later. Alligator, with high blue value and moderate red value, will be opportunistic: buying red or blue if the price in green is attractive, but also possibly selling some red for green if offers are very good. Three-player uncertainty adds competition: when a proposer posts a favorable trade, both non-senders may want it, and random selection among acceptors makes it impossible to target a specific partner. This competition can induce slightly more generous offers than pure bilateral bargaining, and some strategic responses (e.g., declining a marginally good trade if one thinks a better one is likely next round). Over three rounds each player only gets three proposal turns, so they balance exploration with the desire to lock in positive-surplus deals before the game ends.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, no trades have occurred, so beliefs about others' red/blue values are very diffuse. Lion, as a patient and exploratory proposer with low valuations for both red and blue, is likely to test the waters by offering a small quantity of her low-value chips for green, modestly shading terms in her favor. Iguana then gets a turn, armed with her high value for blue and moderate value for red; she will likely propose a trade that acquires red or blue at a seemingly reasonable price in green, expecting at least one counterpart to accept. Alligator, valuing blue highly and red moderately, will likely respond opportunistically, accepting any trades that improve her dollar payoff while using her own proposal to either buy more high-value blue or sell lower-valued red if a deal is available.

Going into round 1, several profitable trades have already occurred, giving players partial signals: lion has successfully sold red for green, revealing some willingness of others to hold red; alligator has both bought blue from iguana and sold red to iguana, suggesting that both counterparts see some value in red/blue and are willing to move green. Inventories are now uneven, so proposals must respect changed holdings. Lion, encouraged by her earlier success and still valuing red/blue low, is likely to continue converting red into green at slightly tougher terms. Iguana, having parted with some green but gained red and lost blue, will seek to rebalance—ideally regaining blue or strengthening her red position if she can do so without overpaying. Alligator, now with more blue and fewer red and green, will be selective: she wants to preserve her remaining green while possibly monetizing excess blue if someone appears to undervalue it, or grabbing cheap red if lion continues to offer favorable rates.

By round 2, the final proposal opportunities, each player has seen the others' revealed willingness to trade red and blue against green. Lion has already transformed much of her low-value red into green and now has little remaining red to sell; she can make one last attempt to squeeze value out of remaining red if any, or stand pat if terms would be poor. Iguana has accumulated considerable red and traded away multiple chunks of green and some blue; with high values on both red and blue, she may now focus on small, safe improvements or decline marginal trades to avoid giving up scarce green. Alligator has ended up relatively rich in blue and moderate in red, with reduced green; she will only accept trades that clearly move her toward more green or preserve her strong blue position. Late in the game, because there is no future beyond round 2, players are less interested in experimentation and more in locking in only clearly positive-surplus moves, making declined offers more likely if terms are not obviously attractive.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips ($0.50 for everyone) and privately valued red/blue chips. All players start with 10 of each chip type. Red chips are most valuable to whale ($0.70) and moderately valuable to dolphin ($0.50) but relatively weak for duck ($0.20). Blue chips are modestly valuable for whale ($0.30) and lower for dolphin and duck ($0.20 each). Because green is worth the same to all, it mainly functions as a stable medium of exchange when one player wants to offload a color that is low-value to them but high-value to someone else. The main efficiency gains come from whale acquiring red chips from duck (and to a lesser extent from dolphin), potentially paying in blue or green, and from dolphin trading away blue for red or green. Duck is generally on the sell side for red and blue and has little reason to buy those colors, as they are low value to her relative to green. All three players have low to very low acceptance thresholds and relatively low exploitation caution, so they are predisposed to agree to any trade that shows a visible positive gain on their calculator, even if the counterpart gains more. Dolphin is low-search, short-horizon, and tolerant of unequal outcomes, so he is likely to make simple, slightly self-favoring but still positive-sum offers and accept many incoming opportunities. Whale is somewhat more exploratory and socially adaptive, likely to test multi-chip trades that give clear gains to both sides and especially to accumulate red while spending blue and green. Duck is a cooperative, low-competition, low-search player likely to accept most offers that show any gain, with relatively little attempt to push for better terms. The main uncertainty and strategic friction come from (1) incomplete information about others’ exact red/blue values, which can lead to conservative pricing, especially early; (2) the random assignment of recipient when both non-senders accept, which makes it risky to post very generous terms that two parties would jump on; and (3) limited bookkeeping precision, which may lead players to favor round numbers and small trades. Over three rounds with one proposal per player per round, the most likely pattern is early exploratory but obviously beneficial trades (especially whale buying red from duck), followed by incremental adjustments as players see who is hungry for which colors. Given all three are deal-ready and green is common-value, we expect multiple accepted trades, relatively little hard bargaining, and final holdings with whale holding more red and fewer blue/green, duck holding more green and fewer red, and dolphin somewhere in between, often trading away blue for better-valued colors.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, players have symmetric starting inventories and no public information about others’ private red/blue values. Proposals are likely to be conservative, small, and framed using the common-value green chip as a safe baseline. Each player will test the waters by offering trades that the calculator shows as a clear gain for themselves but are plausibly also attractive for at least one counterpart. Because everyone’s acceptance thresholds are low and search tendencies are modest to low, early clearly-positive offers have a high chance of being taken. Dolphin opens, probably with a simple red–green or blue–green trade; whale then has an opportunity to start accumulating red; duck, going last, may either follow observed patterns or post a straightforward improvement trade based on what she infers others like to buy or sell.

By the start of round 1, players have observed one executed trade: whale bought red from duck in exchange for blue. This publicly reveals that whale is willing to pay blue to acquire red and that duck is willing to part with red for blue at a modestly favorable rate. Dolphin infers that red is probably especially valuable to whale and at least somewhat more valuable than blue to duck, though the exact valuations remain unknown. With this structure, dolphin now has a clearer target if he wants to trade red for blue or green; whale is incentivized to keep building red holdings, leaning into blue as a funding source; and duck knows her red is in demand and may be more open to converting it into green if she sees a simple, positive-sum offer. Given the low search and low thresholds, players are still biased toward small, clean trades that plainly improve their own payoffs without requiring fine-grained optimization.

Entering the final round, patterns are clearer: whale has repeatedly demonstrated strong demand for red and readiness to pay with both blue and green; duck has been willing to sell red for either blue or green when the calculator shows any gain; and dolphin has been a somewhat hesitant but opportunistic accepter, especially when he can cheaply acquire green or unload blue. With only one proposal left for each player, strategic patience matters less; the dominant behavior is to propose any trade that yields an immediate gain and is plausibly attractive to at least one counterpart. Because all three have low acceptance thresholds and are aware that this is the last chance to improve payoffs, final-round offers tilt toward slightly more generous, obviously beneficial terms designed to secure at least one acceptance rather than finely tuned surplus division.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with symmetric chip inventories (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN) and know only their own dollar values. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, while RED and BLUE are idiosyncratic: zebra values RED highly ($0.80) and BLUE moderately ($0.30); gorilla values BLUE moderately ($0.40) and RED low ($0.20); turtle values BLUE very highly ($0.70) and RED very low ($0.10). This creates strong, complementary gains from trade: zebra wants to shed BLUE and acquire RED/possibly GREEN; gorilla wants BLUE and GREEN, is happy to give up RED; turtle really wants BLUE, is happy to dump RED. Many Pareto-improving trades (e.g., RED from gorilla/turtle to zebra in exchange for BLUE, or GREEN for RED/BLUE) exist. However, each player is ignorant of others’ valuations and only infers them through proposals and acceptances.

Behaviorally, zebra is cooperative, less guarded, and has a low acceptance threshold; gorilla is also deal-ready, tolerant of unequal outcomes, and somewhat exploratory; turtle is more numerate and state-disciplined but low-search and quick-deal oriented with a somewhat higher acceptance threshold than the others. This implies the group is relatively willing to transact early if proposals show clear private surplus. Random tie-breaking among multiple accepters constrains targeted partner choice, so proposers must offer terms that they are comfortable executing with either potential recipient. Because everyone sees their own net surplus before deciding, they are unlikely to accept any weak or negative-surplus deal; but low thresholds mean they will accept even modest positive gains rather than hold out for speculative better terms.

Across three rounds (nine proposal turns), a likely pattern is: (1) early, somewhat conservative, near-fair trades as the players feel out valuations, especially around GREEN (the only common-value chip); (2) mid-game, more targeted exploitation of comparative advantages in RED/BLUE once at least one player infers that others are shedding low-value chips; and (3) late-game, quick, relatively myopic surplus grabs rather than elaborate multi-step positioning, consistent with limited rounds and the players’ mostly low patience and low guard. Zebra and gorilla are inclined to propose generous-enough swaps to get quick acceptances, while turtle uses disciplined calculations to avoid obvious exploitation but still takes clear gains. Overall, the game likely features several accepted trades, modest but positive individual gains, and no full exploitation of all theoretical surplus due to limited information and short horizon.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, no one has seen any behavior yet; players only know their own valuations. They are likely to begin with relatively symmetric, low-risk proposals that create obvious personal gains while being plausibly acceptable to at least one counterpart. Because GREEN is common-value and focal, early offers may involve GREEN to build trust and test responsiveness. Zebra, as the first proposer and low-aggressiveness type, is inclined to offer terms that are clearly positive for herself yet not obviously exploitative. Gorilla, with very low acceptance threshold and exploratory tendencies, may quickly lock in a favorable trade once she sees a clear positive surplus, especially involving moving low-value RED out for GREEN or BLUE. Turtle, while more cautious and numerate, is still quick-deal oriented and will accept clean, high-surplus opportunities that align with her strong preference for BLUE over RED.

By the start of round 1, each player has seen that others are willing to trade and has some weak evidence about relative preferences: zebra has signaled some willingness to give up BLUE for GREEN, gorilla has just exchanged low-value RED for medium-value BLUE and then taken in more BLUE, and turtle has aggressively dumped RED for BLUE. Public inventories now show gorilla with reduced RED and increased BLUE, turtle with reduced RED and increased BLUE, and zebra with slightly fewer BLUE and more GREEN. This pattern hints that RED might be especially low value for gorilla and turtle, and that BLUE is attractive to both gorilla and turtle. Zebra may infer that her high-value RED is in demand or at least that others are not hoarding it; she might try to obtain RED from gorilla or turtle at favorable rates. Gorilla, seeing turtle’s strong appetite for BLUE, may propose further RED-for-BLUE trades, while turtle, having secured substantial BLUE already, may look for one or two more clean surplus deals but is less inclined to experiment widely due to low search tendencies.

Entering the final round, players have a clearer revealed pattern: gorilla and turtle have repeatedly accepted trades that convert their low-value RED into higher-value BLUE, and zebra has executed at least one trade to acquire underpriced RED from gorilla. Inventories are now skewed: zebra holds more RED and GREEN and fewer BLUE; gorilla holds few RED and many BLUE; turtle also holds many BLUE and relatively few RED. With only three proposal turns left, strategic patience is limited. Zebra’s best marginal gains come from shedding residual BLUE or even some GREEN if counterpart valuations justify it, in exchange for any remaining cheap RED from gorilla or turtle. Gorilla, with very low acceptance thresholds and a large BLUE stock, is still happy to swap remaining RED or even GREEN if she perceives a surplus, but is becoming inventory-constrained on RED. Turtle, having already realized substantial gains by offloading RED, has diminishing opportunities but will still take obviously positive, simple trades. The final round is therefore characterized by small, high-probability surplus trades rather than bold experiments, with everyone prioritizing straightforward value increases over complex positioning.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features heterogeneous private values for red and blue chips, with common-value green chips. All players start with 10 of each color. Because everyone values green at $0.50, green functions as a transparent, neutral currency: any trade involving green is easy for all to evaluate. By contrast, red and blue have different private values, creating scope for surplus-generating trades when a chip is more valuable to the buyer than to the seller.

Private values:
- cat-green-4185 (Cat): RED=$0.50, BLUE=$0.70, GREEN=$0.50 (values blue the most; indifferent between red and green).
- iguana-yellow-3262 (Iguana): RED=$0.80, BLUE=$0.30, GREEN=$0.50 (values red highly; blue is low-value for her).
- zebra-pink-2457 (Zebra): RED=$0.40, BLUE=$0.80, GREEN=$0.50 (values blue highly; red is lowest for her).

Thus, efficient directions of trade are:
- Iguana should buy red and sell blue.
- Cat and Zebra should buy blue and potentially sell red.
- All three are indifferent regarding green vs. their own red or vs. each other’s green, but they may use green as a known yardstick or sweetener.

Strategically, Cat is a deal-ready, low-threshold accepter with solid bookkeeping but relatively low exploitation caution; he’s likely to propose and accept trades that yield even small positive gains, without pushing terms too hard. Iguana is numerically disciplined, moderately exploratory, with a somewhat low acceptance threshold but higher uncertainty aversion; she will seek clearly positive, structured deals, especially ones that convert her low-value blue into high-value red. Zebra is also deal-ready and relatively unguarded, with moderate patience but weaker numeracy; she may sometimes accept slightly dominated deals if they appear decent, but her low exploitation caution and low threshold mean she often takes modest gains rather than waiting.

Uncertainty arises because each player only sees their own valuations. They infer others’ values from observed proposals and acceptance patterns, but in a short three-round game there is limited time to learn. Proposers must design offers that are: (1) profitable given their own values, (2) likely to be non-negative for at least one other player despite not knowing that player’s chip values, and (3) robust to the fact that if multiple others accept, the partner is chosen at random. Given all three are relatively tolerant of unequal splits and care more about securing a gain than enforcing fairness, the likely bargaining dynamics emphasize practical, surplus-creating trades using obvious win-win structure (e.g., Iguana trading blue for red, Zebra trading red for blue) rather than strategic holdout.

Across three rounds with each player proposing once per round, we can expect early offers to be somewhat exploratory in structure and conservatively favorable to the proposer. As players see who accepts which color directions (e.g., who jumps at red-for-blue or green-for-red), they refine later proposals. Because all players have low to moderate acceptance thresholds and a surplus calculator, several trades will likely be accepted, especially when they clearly move chips toward higher-value owners while leaving each side non-negative. However, some attempts may still fail when the proposer misjudges the counterpart’s relative values or offers too thin a margin for a risk-averse or patient responder.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, players have no direct evidence about others’ specific chip values. They will likely start with simple, low-risk proposals that reflect their own priorities: Iguana wants more red and fewer blue; Cat and Zebra want more blue and can spare some red. Proposals may be modest in size, probing which directions counterparts are willing to trade, with everyone watching acceptance patterns to infer who values which colors.

By the second round, players have observed that Zebra eagerly accepted trades that increased her blue holdings and that Iguana was willing to give up blue for red, revealing their rough preferences. Cat has slightly improved his blue position but also seen that Zebra is an active trade partner. Now proposers can craft more targeted, surplus-sharing deals: Iguana can continue converting blue to red, and Cat and Zebra can both seek further blue gains or exploit known directions of counterpart demand. Some proposals may be a bit bolder in terms, but all three still face the random-recipient risk when both others might accept.

In the final round, everyone has clearer signals: Iguana consistently buys red for blue; Zebra grabs blue even at somewhat costly terms; Cat is willing to make small, positive trades in both red–blue and red–green dimensions. With limited time left, proposers focus on cementing final, incremental gains rather than holding out for perfect terms. Risk of multi-acceptance remains, but prior behavior suggests that most offers targeted toward a specific known preference (e.g., giving blue to Iguana or Zebra) are likely to be accepted by that intended partner while the other party often declines if the trade direction is misaligned with their values.
