parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features privately known values on red and blue chips, with green chips known to be worth $0.50 to everyone. All players begin with symmetric inventories (10 of each color), but they have different preferences over red and blue, which create potential gains from trade. Bear values BLUE the highest ($1.00) and also likes RED ($0.70); Peacock has relatively low values on both BLUE ($0.40) and RED ($0.60); Swan has a strong preference for BLUE ($0.90) and a relatively low value on RED ($0.50). Since GREEN is common-value at $0.50, it serves as a natural medium of exchange when a player wants to pull more of their high-value color from someone else.

Incentives: Bear and Swan both want to accumulate BLUE and are comparatively okay using RED as currency. Peacock is the least attached to BLUE and moderately attached to RED, so Peacock is a natural seller of BLUE and, to a lesser degree, a buyer of RED when the price is favorable in terms of GREEN. Because accept decisions are individual and simultaneous, a proposer who offers surplus to both counterparts risks having multiple acceptances and a random counterpart. That uncertainty incentivizes proposers to craft deals that are clearly more attractive to one specific counterpart or at least neutral-to-bad for the other. The UI shows each player their exact net payoff from any proposed trade, so acceptance behavior is guided by clear dollar gains instead of rough heuristics.

Behaviorally, all three personas are low-threshold, deal-ready accepters with relatively low exploitation caution, which means they are inclined to take any trade that yields a noticeable positive gain, even if it is somewhat unbalanced. Bear is moderately patient and likes predictability, so she is likely to pursue clearly positive but not necessarily extreme deals and avoid overly complex or high-variance plays. Peacock is patient and highly exploratory, willing to test the space with several different offers and to accept small but safe gains. Swan is numerate, exploratory, and also low-threshold, so she is likely to compute the gains carefully, accept thin-margin wins, and propose trades that squeeze a bit more surplus if she sees it. Across three short rounds, the main dynamics will center on Bear and Swan trying to buy BLUE and sell RED, with Peacock often acting as a flexible middle player, sometimes selling BLUE for GREEN and sometimes trading GREEN for cheap RED. Random recipient selection when multiple parties accept introduces modest uncertainty about exactly who trades with whom, but because everyone is fairly tolerant and oriented toward making deals, we should see a small number of mostly efficient trades that shift BLUE toward Bear and Swan and some RED/green moving as payment.

Given only three rounds and one proposal per player per round, players cannot finely optimize inventories, but they can quickly grab obvious arbitrage opportunities: for example, Peacock selling BLUE to Bear or Swan at an implicit price between $0.40 and $0.90, or Bear trading RED for GREEN or BLUE at rates that remain profitable from her perspective. The key tensions are (1) how aggressively proposers push terms in their own favor versus keeping them clearly acceptable, and (2) how often more than one responder finds the same offer attractive, creating random matching risk. With these personas, the likely outcome is a modest number of mutually profitable but somewhat lopsided deals rather than hard bargaining standoffs.

rounds.round_explanation: Everyone starts with 10 of each chip color and only knows their own private values. In the first round, players are feeling out what kinds of rates might be acceptable. Because they cannot see others' values, they will tentatively test simple one-color-for-another offers at modest ratios that look clearly profitable from their own perspective. Bear opens the game with a conservative, value-positive offer. Peacock, as a patient searcher, is likely to look for a slightly better margin in his own proposal while still leaving clear gains to at least one counterpart. Swan, with strong numeracy and low acceptance threshold, is inclined to accept any proposal that yields her a clear positive gain, even if it's small, and in her own turn she will probably construct deals that tilt slightly in her favor but are still likely to be accepted.

By the second round, the players have seen a few concrete exchange rates and updated their beliefs about others’ willingness to trade. Bear has successfully acquired some BLUE and given up some RED, signaling that RED is a workable payment medium. Peacock has revealed a willingness to sell BLUE and buy RED and to use GREEN as a flexible chip. Swan has shown that she will accept modest but clear gains in both buying RED and selling BLUE. Now players can target more specific improvements: Bear can keep converting RED into BLUE or perhaps GREEN at favorable terms, Peacock can arbitrage between RED and BLUE using GREEN, and Swan can continue to buy BLUE or cheap RED while unloading GREEN she values only at the common rate. Proposals in this round become slightly more tailored, but they are still simple single-color-for-single-color offers.

In the final round, inventories have shifted: Bear and Swan now hold more BLUE and somewhat less RED, while Peacock has accumulated additional RED and perhaps a bit of GREEN. Everyone anticipates that no further rounds remain, so they focus on locking in any remaining positive-sum opportunities. Deals that were marginal earlier may now be acceptable if they tidy up portfolios with even small gains. Bear, still valuing BLUE most, may attempt one last BLUE-for-RED or BLUE-for-GREEN trade. Peacock, as a flexible middle player, may attempt to offload low-value BLUE for higher-value RED or GREEN. Swan may use her final proposal to trim excess RED or GREEN in exchange for BLUE if she can get it. Because all three have low acceptance thresholds and a bias toward getting something rather than nothing in the last round, we expect more offers to be accepted if they show even modest positive gains to at least one counterpart.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on exchanging privately valued red and blue chips plus common-value green chips, under inventory constraints and a fixed three-round horizon. Each player starts with 10 of each color. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, but red and blue are asymmetric: Gorilla and Monkey both value red at $1.00, while Camel values red at only $0.30; Camel and Monkey both value blue highly ($0.60 and $0.90, respectively), while Gorilla values blue at only $0.10. This creates strong two-sided gains from trade: Camel wants to turn cheap red into higher-value blue and green, Gorilla wants to turn cheap blue into expensive red and keep green, and Monkey finds both red and blue valuable but has especially high value for blue, so he is a natural buyer of blue and reluctant seller of it. Because each proposal must be a simple bilateral exchange of one color for a different color, the players must structure trades that each side sees as individually profitable, while also dealing with the rule that if multiple recipients accept, a partner is chosen at random. Gorilla is patient, low-search, and has a low acceptance threshold, so she is likely to propose simple, safe, mildly self-favoring trades and accept any offer with even a modest positive gain. Camel is numerate and disciplined with low acceptance threshold and moderate aggressiveness: he will compute net gains carefully, accept good deals even if somewhat unbalanced, and aim to convert his low-value red into blue or green. Monkey is strategic, high-search, and more selective; he will explore but insist on reasonably mutual deals, especially avoiding giving up too much of his high-value blue without adequate compensation. Uncertainty comes mainly from incomplete information about others' chip values and from the random selection of a recipient when both potential partners would accept a deal. Over three rounds, we expect early proposals to be exploratory, gradually revealing where value discrepancies lie (e.g., Gorilla's eagerness to give up blue or Camel's eagerness to give up red). Because the horizon is short and all three are willing to accept positive-surplus deals, we anticipate several trades executing, especially between Camel and each of the high-red-value players, and between Gorilla and Camel around blue-for-red. Monkey's higher selectivity will limit how many times he agrees to sell blue, but he will readily buy red cheaply if offered generous terms. The observed path below is a plausible single trajectory consistent with these incentives and personas, with some exploratory offers being rejected and other clearly profitable cross-value trades being accepted.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, all players are at their symmetric 10-10-10 holdings. Gorilla has strong reasons to acquire red and to offload blue; Camel has strong reasons to offload red and acquire blue; Monkey values both red and blue highly but is especially attached to blue. None of them yet know the others' private values, so initial proposals are likely to be exploratory and somewhat conservative. Gorilla, as a patient but low-search proposer with a low acceptance threshold, will start with a simple trade that gives her a solid gain in expected value while not being so extreme that both potential recipients reject it. Camel, numerate and opportunistic, will then try to push a more self-serving but still positive-sum conversion of his weak red into stronger assets. Monkey, analytically strong and search-oriented, will likely test the waters with a carefully chosen trade that protects his high blue value while trying to pick up underpriced red.

Entering Round 1, only one trade has executed: Camel traded 2 GREEN for 3 RED with Monkey. Camel has increased his low-value red holdings, but from his own perspective, this actually sacrificed some common-value green; Monkey gained red that he values highly in exchange for neutral-value green. This suggests Camel slightly misjudged relative valuations, while Monkey took a favorable if modest gain. Gorilla, having had her first offer refused, now knows that neither counterpart was eager to give up green cheaply, hinting that green may be common-value or at least not low for them. She may pivot toward offering or requesting blue instead. Camel, having just completed a trade with Monkey, now better infers that Monkey values red but may have underestimated how much; he will try to convert some of his increasing stock of cheap red into the more valuable blue. Monkey, after seeing Gorilla's failed attempt to buy green and Camel's willingness to part with green, infers Gorilla and Camel both value green around its face value; he will aim to sell green or maybe a tiny bit of blue only when return is strong, while continuing to accumulate red at favorable rates.

By Round 2, some of the underlying structure is clearer to the players, even if they do not know exact values. Gorilla has successfully swapped some of her low-value blue for higher-value red, especially via Camel, suggesting to her that Camel values blue more than red. Camel has gradually converted cheap red into blue by trading with Monkey, learning that Monkey values blue a lot and is hesitant to part with it unless heavily compensated. Monkey has accumulated a significant amount of red through favorable exchanges from both Camel and earlier from Camel via green, reinforcing his strategy of buying red whenever the terms are good. At this late stage, with only three proposal turns remaining, all players are more willing to finalize clearly beneficial trades and less inclined to experiment. Gorilla aims for one last improvement in her red holdings by offloading remaining blue; Camel still seeks to rebalance away from his still-cheap red toward valuable blue and possibly green; Monkey, strategically patient but now facing the end of the game, is willing to accept any cleanly positive deals that do not strip him of too much blue, while proposing at least one more attempt to trade low-value assets for higher ones.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
Each player starts with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so it is a safe, neutral asset. The main differences are in RED and BLUE:
- alligator-yellow-2789 (Alligator): BLUE=$0.60, GREEN=$0.50, RED=$0.10
- otter-green-8822 (Otter): BLUE=$1.00, GREEN=$0.50, RED=$0.40
- tiger-red-5171 (Tiger): BLUE=$0.90, GREEN=$0.50, RED=$0.70

This creates strong, but asymmetric, incentives:
- Alligator badly wants to turn low-value RED ($0.10) into either GREEN ($0.50) or BLUE ($0.60). Almost any trade where they give RED and receive BLUE or GREEN is attractive. They are prosocial, low-aggressiveness, low acceptance threshold, and low search, so they will likely propose and accept mild win–win trades quickly.
- Otter values BLUE the highest at $1.00 and has moderate value for RED ($0.40). Because of very low acceptance thresholds and high search, Otter is willing to accept small gains and to explore many configurations, but is quite anchor-sensitive. They will try to accumulate BLUE cheaply (especially from Alligator, for whom BLUE is only $0.60) while being flexible about giving GREEN.
- Tiger has the highest RED value at $0.70 and high BLUE ($0.90). They are careful with bookkeeping and somewhat more selective than the other two, with moderate exploitation caution. Tiger is willing to trade when the terms are clearly beneficial, but less likely to grab tiny gains than Otter or Alligator.

Given that each proposal must involve offering one color and requesting a different color, the most natural mutually beneficial directions are:
- Alligator: trade away RED for BLUE or GREEN; possibly later trade away some GREEN for BLUE if they perceive BLUE as slightly better.
- Otter: trade away GREEN for BLUE; possibly buy some RED cheaply if terms are good, but RED is less attractive than BLUE.
- Tiger: trade away GREEN to get more RED or BLUE, favoring RED because of the higher value.

Key uncertainties:
- No one knows others' valuations, only that BLUE and RED are somewhere between $0.10 and $1.00. So they do not know, for example, that Tiger values RED so highly or that Alligator values RED so little.
- Because multiple acceptances trigger random partner selection, proposers cannot target a specific counterparty. They must choose a rate that is acceptable to at least one partner but not so generous that both pile in unexpectedly.
- The game lasts only 3 rounds (9 total proposal turns), so players may become more willing to take small positive-sum trades in later rounds rather than hold out for better terms.

Given the personas, we expect:
- Alligator to initiate gentle, clearly positive offers that offload RED and to accept many favorable incoming trades, sometimes even if the gain is modest.
- Otter to experiment with rates, quickly locking in any clearly positive GREEN-for-BLUE or GREEN-for-RED trade, prioritizing BLUE accumulation.
- Tiger to accept only clearly beneficial terms and to protect their valuable RED while using GREEN as the main currency they are willing to give up.

The forecast below assumes players gravitate toward simple, integer-sized exchanges that obviously improve their own payoffs, and that in ambiguous cases they aim for roughly "fair but slightly self-favoring" terms.

rounds.round_explanation: Everyone starts symmetric in quantities but asymmetric in values. Players are still guessing about others' preferences, so first-round offers will likely be simple and exploratory. Alligator, with low acceptance threshold and low search, will try an easy, obviously good RED-for-GREEN trade. Otter will likely push a bit harder to get valuable BLUE or RED using GREEN. Tiger will start conservatively, likely offering GREEN for either RED or BLUE at a rate that clearly benefits them but is not extreme, probing who is more willing to trade.

After round 0, everyone has seen that modestly favorable GREEN-for-RED and GREEN-for-BLUE trades can be agreed quickly. Alligator now holds fewer RED and slightly more BLUE and GREEN, having already improved their portfolio; they may still seek to dump remaining low-value RED. Otter has shifted some GREEN into both BLUE and RED and has learned that both counterparts will trade GREEN for colored chips at non-punishing ratios. Tiger has learned that Otter is particularly willing to trade GREEN for RED. Round 1 will likely feature slightly more directional, less exploratory offers: Alligator keeps unloading RED, Otter continues to exchange GREEN for BLUE, and Tiger again uses GREEN to buy more RED from whoever is willing.

By the final round, everyone has multiple data points that GREEN can be reliably swapped for RED or BLUE at gentle rates. With only three proposals left, players are inclined to take any clearly positive trade, even small. parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game has strong scope for mutually beneficial trades because players value chips very differently. Green chips are common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so they act as a safe, stable currency. Red and blue chips are private-value: frog-yellow-5901 (frog) values red very highly ($0.90) and blue highly ($0.50); pig-green-5362 (pig) values red moderately ($0.40) and blue modestly ($0.30); dolphin-green-1598 (dolphin) values both red and blue very little ($0.10) but is patient and exploratory. This creates clear gains-from-trade patterns: dolphin has a strong incentive to trade away red/blue in exchange for green; frog wants to acquire red in particular and is willing to part with green; pig is flexible and relatively accepting, and will often take even small gains. Behavioral priors matter: frog and pig both have very low acceptance thresholds and low exploitation caution, so they will frequently accept offers that give them any positive surplus, even if the proposer benefits more. Dolphin is more patient and exploratory, but less aggressive, and is likely to craft deals that give counterparts visible gains so they accept, while still improving her own position by converting low-value chips into green. Because responders see exact net gains in dollars, they won’t knowingly accept negative-surplus trades. Randomization over multiple acceptors can make some proposals risky for proposers if they want a specific partner, but in this profile most trades that give positive surplus to both non-senders are likely to attract multiple acceptances. Over the three rounds, we should see dolphin steadily trade away low-value red/blue for green, frog aggressively try to buy red using green (and sometimes blue) even on less favorable terms, and pig opportunistically accept nearly any positive deal, with some multi-acceptor randomization early that shapes later holdings. By the end, all three should have improved their total dollar value, with frog holding more red, dolphin more green, and pig somewhere in between with a mix that reflects whatever randomization occurred.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players have symmetric chip counts (10 of each color) but asymmetric values. Dolphin knows her red and blue are almost worthless to her and will look to turn them into green, but she does not know that frog and pig value red and blue more. Frog knows red is especially valuable and will be inclined to use green to acquire red, but she also values blue highly and will not want to overpay in her own terms. Pig is deal-ready and less numerically meticulous, so he’s likely to accept any clear small profit without much strategic delay. Because acceptance thresholds for frog and pig are low, even mildly one-sided offers in their favor are likely to be accepted. This first round is about discovering who is willing to trade and what directions of trade seem easy to clear: dolphin tentatively tests a green-for-red exchange, frog tries to buy red with green from the more flexible pig, and pig then uses his new red position to extract additional gains from dolphin’s low-value red/blue holding.

Going into round 1, holdings have shifted. Dolphin traded away some low-value red for green, modestly improving her position. Frog successfully acquired red from pig but paid full common-value green, so from frog’s perspective the trade was still good due to her high red value. Pig is now holding more green and less red than at the start, having sold red twice and bought green once. The updated incentives: dolphin still wants to shed red and even blue for green; frog now has an even stronger appetite for more red but somewhat fewer greens; pig, flush with green, is in a position to either buy back some red or blue cheaply or sell green if he gets compensated adequately in his own valuations. With early positive interactions behind them, all players are now confident that deals will clear if they give responders clear surplus. This round sees dolphin making a more ambitious multi-chip gain attempt, frog trying to exploit her high red value further, and pig testing whether he can cheaply acquire blue from dolphin.

By round 2, several profitable trades have occurred, and each player has seen others accept small but positive-surplus deals. Dolphin has converted a substantial share of her low-value red and blue into green; frog has accumulated valuable red at the cost of some green and blue; pig has cycled between red, blue, and green, generally picking up small margins by accepting favorable offers. With only one round left, strategic patience matters less, and players become more willing to lock in even small additional gains rather than holding out for ideal terms. Dolphin will still exchange any remaining low-value chips for green at acceptable rates, frog will fine-tune her portfolio by swapping residual green or blue for red if possible, and pig will opportunistically accept anything that increases his payoff. This final round features smaller, tidying trades, with each proposer targeting the counterpart most likely to accept, and trade sizes shrinking as inventory constraints and diminishing marginal gains limit further big moves.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips ($0.50 each for everyone) and private-value red and blue chips. Owl values red the most (RED=$1.00), squid slightly less (RED=$0.90), and iguana least (RED=$0.30). For blue chips, owl again has the highest value (BLUE=$0.60), squid is second (BLUE=$0.50), and iguana is lowest (BLUE=$0.20). Green is symmetric at $0.50 for all players.

The key surplus opportunities are: (1) shifting red chips away from iguana toward owl or squid, and (2) shifting blue chips away from iguana toward owl or squid, and potentially from squid toward owl if the terms are attractive to squid. Green chips generate no surplus from trading because everyone values them equally, but they are useful as a neutral medium of exchange and a way to disguise private values. All players start with 10 of each chip, so no one is constrained initially.

Iguana has strong bookkeeping discipline, moderate patience, and relatively low exchange aggressiveness. He is likely to calculate net payoffs carefully and prefer modest, win–win trades rather than very tough terms. Because he values both red and blue less than the others, he is a natural low-cost supplier of those chips and will often try to sell them for green or for a smaller number of red/blue chips that still increase his dollar surplus.

Owl is a deal-ready accepter with very low acceptance thresholds, low search, and low exploitation caution. She highly values both red and blue and dislikes uncertainty, so she is prone to accept any trade that gives her even a small positive gain, especially if it immediately increases her count of red or blue chips. She is less likely to exploit information about her own high valuations strategically and more likely to grab simple, clearly beneficial deals.

Squid is also relatively deal-ready with low–moderate acceptance thresholds and mixed aggressiveness. She values red and blue quite highly, though slightly less than owl. She is willing to accept positive-gain trades but has more moderate guardrails than owl and somewhat more exploration, so she may hold out if a trade feels too lopsided.

The main uncertainty is about what each player believes regarding others’ values. No one sees others’ private valuations, so they infer them slowly from observed acceptance behavior. Early in the game, proposals will likely be conservative and framed around obviously value-increasing swaps using green chips as payment. As the three rounds proceed, players may become slightly bolder if they see fast acceptances. With only three rounds (nine total turns), there is limited time to wait for perfect terms, which interacts with owl’s and squid’s low thresholds to produce a relatively high likelihood of quick agreements whenever a trade offers clear surplus to the responder, even if it is not globally efficient.

Because responders move simultaneously and the partner is randomized if both accept, proposers cannot target the most favorable partner with certainty. This pushes senders to frame trades that are at least weakly attractive to both potential responders or slightly biased toward the higher-probability accepter (here, typically owl). Overall, we should expect iguana to initiate modest, win–win trades to monetize his low-valued red/blue; owl to snap up beneficial opportunities even if small; and squid to accept many, but not all, positive offers while occasionally making her own surplus-improving proposals when her turn comes.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has seen any bargaining behavior yet, so proposals are likely to be conservative and use green chips as a neutral medium. Iguana, moving first, will try a safe, modest trade that slightly improves his position by selling a higher-valued chip to a counterpart in exchange for green. Owl is very ready to accept small positive gains and may test a trade that uses her relatively neutral green chips to acquire higher-valued red or blue from iguana or squid. Squid, going last, will observe both previous turns and may either piggyback on the structure she has seen or try a simple surplus-positive swap that she expects at least owl to accept.

By round 1, both owl and squid have revealed a willingness to accept modest but positive trades involving green for more valuable red or blue. Iguana has seen owl accept a 1 GREEN for 1 BLUE deal and should infer that owl values blue above green, confirming that selling blue for green was good for him. Owl has also seen squid accept GREEN-for-RED and GREEN-for-BLUE trades, signaling similar preferences. Now, proposers can be slightly more confident that simple 1-for-1 trades involving green versus a more valuable color will be accepted. Iguana will likely continue to monetize his undervalued blue; owl may repeat her earlier pattern of using green to acquire red, and squid may again seek to turn green into blue.

In the final round, everyone has a clear pattern: iguana repeatedly sells low-valued blue for green, owl reliably uses green to buy higher-valued red or blue, and squid converts green into blue when possible. With very few turns remaining, the value of waiting declines, and low-threshold players like owl and squid favor closing any remaining positive-sum deals. Iguana will likely stick with the safe, previously accepted structure. Owl and squid, having seen prior acceptances, can confidently repeat similar trades that keep generating small but sure gains.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player, three-round bargaining game features privately valued red and blue chips, a common-value green chip, and inventory-constrained one-sided proposals. All players start with 10 of each chip color. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so it is a “safe” benchmark asset that is never strictly dominated by holding cash-equivalents of other chips unless a player’s private value for red/blue is clearly higher. Red chips are high-value for duck ($1), lion ($0.90), and tiger ($1). Blue chips are high-value only to duck ($1), moderate to lion ($0.50), and low to tiger ($0.30). This cross-player mismatch creates the main gains from trade: duck wants to accumulate both red and blue; lion especially wants red but is mostly indifferent between blue and green; tiger wants red but should be relatively happy to give up blue and somewhat cautious about giving up green unless they get strong compensation. However, each player only knows their own valuations, so they must infer others’ values from behavior.

Because proposals are restricted to one color demanded and one color offered, every trade is effectively a bilateral exchange ratio between two chip colors, with the third color’s value serving as an implicit outside option. Players see the exact monetary gain or loss of each proposed trade for themselves via the surplus calculator. This reduces computational mistakes but does not remove uncertainty about others’ valuations or acceptance thresholds. The strategic difficulty is amplified by the rule that if multiple players accept, the recipient is chosen randomly; proposers cannot target a specific partner once multiple acceptances are possible. This discourages extremely generous offers skewed to one suspected counterparty, and encourages somewhat balanced terms that could be profitable with either partner.

Personas suggest: Duck is low-aggressiveness, low-search, numerate, and relatively tolerant of unequal outcomes. Duck is likely to propose modest, near-fair terms that visibly benefit themselves but also show a small gain to any recipient, and to accept decent positive-surplus offers rather than hold out aggressively. Lion is moderately guarded, medium exploration, with a somewhat higher rejection threshold: lion may decline very thin or ambiguous gains, especially if they sense the proposer might be exploiting private information. Tiger is numerate, low-search, and medium-selective: tiger prefers clear positive gains and closure over elaborate multi-round positioning, and is willing to trade when the calculator shows a clean, non-trivial benefit.

Strategically, duck’s double-high valuation (red and blue both at $1) makes them a natural net buyer of both colored chips, especially from tiger (who values blue least) and somewhat from lion. Lion and tiger, both valuing red highly, are unlikely to sell red cheaply; both will view red as their main prize, especially from duck, whose behavior may reveal a strong willingness to pay for red. Since all value green the same at $0.50, green tends to be used as a relatively neutral payment medium, or as a safe store of value for players unsure about others’ private valuations of red/blue.

Given three rounds and one proposal per player per round, the game is short. Early in round 0, players are uncertain and may experiment with slightly conservative, exploratory offers to gauge willingness to trade. Because each proposal adjusts inventories immediately, a successful trade in an early turn can meaningfully reshape incentives later. The most likely patterns are: (1) early probing trades where duck tries to convert green into red/blue, or tests how cheaply they can buy red from lion or tiger; (2) lion and tiger proposing offers that attempt to acquire red from duck or sell blue to duck, while avoiding large green losses; and (3) in later rounds, if inventories have shifted, attempts to consolidate high-value holdings (duck trying to acquire more blue; lion and tiger trying to acquire or protect red) with clearer, more calculated ratios. Declines will occur whenever the surplus gain is very small, when a guarded player fears being the “sucker,” or when an offer sacrifices red without strong compensation. Accepted trades will cluster where the calculator shows an obvious positive monetary gain for the responder, especially if it aligns with their known priorities (e.g., tiger selling blue at a generous rate).

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 begins with symmetric inventories (10 of each color per player) and full uncertainty about others’ private valuations. As first mover, duck is likely to test conservative terms that mildly favor them but still give clear positive surplus to any accepting recipient, especially using green (common-value) to reduce the risk of exploiting someone. Lion, acting second, will observe duck’s opening attempt and may infer that duck values the color they were trying to buy. Lion’s own proposal will likely probe demand for red or test selling low-value chips (blue) at a favorable rate. Tiger, moving third, will see both prior proposals and outcomes; being low-search and moderately selective, tiger is likely to craft a straightforward, profitable offer that either sells blue to someone who appears to value it or buys red at a modest premium if earlier behavior suggests someone undervalues it.

After round 0, inventories have shifted: duck traded away two green for two blue and did not trade red; lion traded three blue for one red with tiger; tiger has become relatively short on blue and slightly reduced in red. These outcomes reveal partial information: duck’s willingness to give up green for blue at a 1:1 rate signals that blue is at least as valuable as green to duck; lion’s acceptance of 3 BLUE for 1 RED signals that lion values red meaningfully more than blue but is not extremely protective of red; tiger’s sale of blue in both trades suggests that blue is low-value to tiger. In round 1, duck will likely continue leveraging their strong valuation for blue and red, trying to acquire more of either from the now-blue-richer lion or from tiger if possible. Lion, now holding more red and fewer blue, may seek to restore some blue or green while preserving most red, and will be wary of selling red too cheaply after giving away one unit in round 0. Tiger, short on blue and down some red, will be more reluctant to sell additional red or green unless the terms are generous, but may still be willing to offload residual blue at good terms or buy red if someone signals undervaluation.

By round 2, prior trades have clarified patterns: duck consistently buys blue and is willing to give up green or even modest amounts of red to do so; lion has shown a willingness to exchange blue for red or green but is cautious about parting with red; tiger repeatedly sells blue and occasionally red when well-compensated. Inventories are now noticeably asymmetric: duck holds a large blue stockpile, modest red, and fewer greens; lion holds more red than initially but fewer blues and greens; tiger has replenished some greens, holds fewer reds than lion and duck, and an intermediate amount of blue. With only three turns remaining, players prioritize consolidating their most valued colors and avoiding trades that sacrifice red without compelling compensation. Duck is likely to make one last attempt to buy red at a generous yet still profitable rate from either counterparty. Lion, on their final proposal, will aim for a clean, safe gain—most likely selling lower-valued blue or trading some green while holding onto red. Tiger, as the final mover, may attempt a larger, more one-sided trade if they feel the others are desperate for certain colors, but the short horizon and revealed preferences will make all players more conservative about accepting thin or risky offers.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. The private values for the other colors are:
- bear-yellow-1294 (Bear): BLUE $1.00, RED $0.20, GREEN $0.50
- goose-green-2832 (Goose): BLUE $0.80, RED $0.80, GREEN $0.50
- ram-blue-9899 (Ram): BLUE $0.80, RED $0.50, GREEN $0.50

From a surplus standpoint, the most efficient direction is to move BLUE chips toward Bear (who values BLUE the most) and RED chips toward Goose (who values RED the most). RED is especially cheap for Bear (only $0.20) and more valuable for both Goose ($0.80) and Ram ($0.50), so Bear gains by trading RED away when she can get BLUE or GREEN in return. Goose is relatively balanced but likes RED a lot and BLUE moderately; Ram sits in between Bear and Goose on RED, and matches Goose on BLUE.

However, each player only knows their own values, not others'. They see that ranges for RED and BLUE are $0.10–$1.00, so they know there is heterogeneity but not where they sit. Given their personas, all three have low acceptance thresholds and are deal-ready: they will usually take any trade that gives them a clear positive gain by their own calculator. Goose and Ram are especially tolerant of unequal deals; Bear is somewhat more patient but still fairly willing to accept small gains. Ram has the strongest numeracy and bookkeeping, so he is more likely to track evolving inventories and to recognize good cross-trades; Goose is more conciliatory, and Bear is somewhat anchor-sensitive with modest numerical discipline.

Given simultaneous private responses and random partner selection if multiple accept, proposers cannot target a specific partner. That tilts them toward terms that are profitable for them regardless of which acceptor they get, and that are still mildly attractive to at least one other player. Low selectivity plus three rounds of three turns each (nine total opportunities) increases the chance that multiple trades go through, even if not all are globally efficient. Early in the game, players experiment with relatively small and safe trades (e.g., 1–3 chips each way) to learn about what others might value from their acceptance behavior. As inventories shift, proposers adjust but still mostly chase local arbitrage from their own perspective, without fully reverse-engineering others’ valuations. We should see Bear primarily trying to sell off RED for BLUE or sometimes GREEN, Goose occasionally swapping GREEN for valued colors (RED/BLUE) but not overly aggressive, and Ram exploring structured deals that improve his mix of BLUE and RED while maintaining enough GREEN as a neutral asset.

Overall, we expect several small to medium accepted trades, some mild competition for attractive offers (leading to random recipient selection), and at least a couple of declined proposals where the proposer either overshoots on price or accidentally crafts a trade that is unattractive to both possible recipients given their own surplus calculators.

rounds.round_explanation: At the very start, all players hold symmetrical inventories (10 of each color) but only know their own values. With low acceptance thresholds and curiosity about what others will take, they are likely to test small, low-risk trades to probe preferences. Bear as first mover will probably try to monetize his cheap RED by asking for a more valuable color (likely BLUE) but may start slightly cautiously to elicit some early acceptance. Goose then responds with her own exploratory proposal, probably aiming to get more RED while giving up GREEN or slightly discounted BLUE. Ram, with strong bookkeeping and high search orientation, will likely craft a first-round proposal that is cleanly positive for himself but not too extreme, to generate at least one early counterparty. In this round, there is high uncertainty, but also high willingness to accept modest positive deals.

By the start of round 1, two trades have executed. Bear has traded away some RED for BLUE, Goose has swapped some GREEN for RED, and Ram has twice accepted as counterparty, signaling that RED and GREEN at those terms were at least mildly attractive to him. Everyone now has noisy but useful information: Bear has seen Ram accept RED-for-BLUE at Bear’s proposed rate; Goose has seen Ram accept GREEN-for-RED. Ram has learned that Bear is willing to offload RED for BLUE and that Goose will pay GREEN for RED. Inventories are modestly unbalanced, but no one is resource-starved. In this middle round, proposers will refine terms: Bear may push slightly harder on selling RED or start to test GREEN; Goose may again try to upgrade GREEN into RED or BLUE, now perhaps in slightly larger quantities; Ram, having already accepted twice, may switch to making a more opportunistic offer that moves his now-surplus RED or GREEN into better-valued colors. Uncertainty is lower, but players still do not know exact valuations, so they continue to use their own surplus calculators and observed behaviors as guides.

Going into the final round, multiple trades have shifted chips toward players who seem to value them more, but inventories are still fairly diversified. Bear now holds a strong BLUE position and has shed some RED and GREEN; Goose has upgraded part of her GREEN into RED and some BLUE but has also parted with RED in a later trade; Ram has been both an active acceptor and proposer, adjusting his portfolio of RED, BLUE, and GREEN in light of earlier terms. With only three proposal opportunities left (one per player), strategic patience gives way to a desire to lock in one last positive trade rather than over-optimizing. Each proposer focuses on clearly positive, modest-sized trades that are likely to get at least one acceptance, rather than stretching for aggressive terms that might be declined. Bear is inclined to move a bit more RED or GREEN for BLUE or at least for neutral GREEN if he feels misbalanced; Goose looks for any remaining chance to cheaply gain valued colors; Ram, with the clearest sense of the evolving price pattern, tries to eke out one more small but safe surplus-increasing swap. Some proposals will still be declined if they accidentally make both recipients worse off, but the low acceptance thresholds and prior experience make at least one more accepted trade likely.

Incentives and styles:
- Butterfly has low acceptance thresholds, low exploitation caution, strong bookkeeping, and high patience. She is willing to accept slightly unbalanced offers if they look like a clear personal gain, and she can track chip counts well. She is surrounded by two players who privately value colored chips more than she does. Given the UI calculator, she will be highly responsive to any offer that shows even a modest gain. As a proposer, she is likely to start with deals that give her obvious positive surplus, even if they are somewhat generous to counterparties, because she is not strongly aggressive.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips and a common known value for green. All start with 10 of each chip, and everyone values green the same at $0.50, so green behaves like a safe, neutral currency in terms of private information. The key hidden-information dimension is how much each player values red vs. blue: bat values blue highly and red weakly; fox values both red and blue strongly with blue highest; frog values red highest and blue weakly. In terms of surplus creation, Pareto-improving trades tend to move blue chips toward bat and fox and move red chips toward fox and frog, often using green sparingly or as liquidity when a direct red–blue swap is hard to structure.

Given all three are "deal-ready accepters" with very low acceptance thresholds and low exploitation caution, we expect relatively frequent acceptances of any trade that strictly improves their own payoff, even if it might be somewhat unbalanced. Their prosocial slant and low norm-enforcement tendencies mean they are unlikely to reject mildly unfavorable-looking deals on fairness grounds; instead, they focus on their own surplus calculator. Bat is numerate and careful, so she will tend to construct clean, surplus-positive proposals that are likely to be accepted. Fox is somewhat less numerate and lower search, and may propose simpler, more direct exchanges that give her a clear gain. Frog is moderately strategic and patient, willing to accept profitable trades and possibly to wait if a proposal seems marginal or zero-gain.

Uncertainty arises because no one knows others' exact red/blue valuations. They see only their own calculator, so they know that a proposed trade is good or bad for themselves but not whether the other side will also gain. As a result, proposals will often be framed conservatively around trades that are strongly profitable for the proposer, without fine-tuning to the recipients' preferences. This can lead to some declined offers despite players' general willingness to trade. Over three rounds, with each player proposing once per round, the typical pattern is: (1) early, exploratory proposals that test simple red↔blue or green-mediated bundles; (2) quick acceptances whenever a respondent sees a clear gain; and (3) a gradual shift toward more targeted exchanges as players infer others' likely values from which offers get accepted. The following forecast traces one plausible full path of play, where players gravitate toward mutually profitable red–blue and red–green swaps that align with their private valuations, with at least one accepted trade in most rounds and bat and fox especially active in creating surplus.

rounds.round_explanation: The opening round starts with all players at 10 of each chip. No one yet has information about others' red/blue values, only the common green value. Bat, as a careful but low-aggressiveness proposer, is likely to float a modest, almost balanced trial that helps her convert low-value red into more useful colors. Fox, who values both red and blue highly, will test a slightly more assertive exchange that nets her valuable red while giving up green she values only at the common rate. Frog, still uninformed about others, will probe for red (his highest-value chip) by offering blue or green at conservative rates. Very low acceptance thresholds and low guardedness make any strictly positive-sum deal likely to be taken, while clearly loss-making deals will be quietly declined via each player's calculator.

By the start of round 1, two trades have executed: bat swapped 3 RED for 2 BLUE with fox, and fox swapped 2 GREEN for 2 RED with frog. Observers now see that bat was eager to give up red for blue, fox was happy to send green for red, and frog welcomed more red. This reveals partial structure: bat likely has low red, high blue; fox and frog both seem to value red. Bat will now try to keep improving her position by selling remaining red or possibly green for blue at rates that stay clearly positive for her. Fox, who now holds more red and slightly less green and blue, sees frog as a willing red buyer and may seek blue from bat using her strong position in red. Frog, short on green and blue but better stocked in red, may switch to acquiring blue cheaply if possible or use small red sales to rebuild green. Everyone remains quite willing to accept any strictly positive personal gain.

Going into the final round, inventories have shifted: bat has systematically offloaded red for blue, fox has become a central trader in red and green while rebuilding blue, and frog has gained red early, then modestly traded it for green. Everyone now has a clearer sense that bat prefers blue over red, fox values both red and blue but will pay green for red, and frog strongly wants red but is also willing to adjust for green. With only three proposal turns left, players are inclined to close any easy, strictly profitable gaps rather than overreach. Bat will look for one last small red→blue improvement if any red remains. Fox will attempt a final tidy exchange, likely giving up a bit of red or green for blue if it yields strong surplus. Frog, with moderate patience and low guardedness, will take any last, clearly positive deal—often a small red→green adjustment that slightly improves his portfolio without risking a loss.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. Dog and Turtle share the same valuation structure (BLUE=$1.00, GREEN=$0.50, RED=$0.50), while Parrot values red slightly more and blue much less (BLUE=$0.30, GREEN=$0.50, RED=$0.60). All begin with 10 of each chip. Because green is common-value and symmetric, it mainly functions as a safe medium of exchange: everyone is indifferent at the margin about its distribution, except as it facilitates trades that convert low-value colors into high-value ones. The core hidden asymmetry is that Dog and Turtle strongly value blue and are relatively indifferent between red and green, while Parrot gains most by acquiring red and is comparatively indifferent to blue. Efficient surplus thus comes from Dog/Turtle converting red to blue and from Parrot converting blue to red, with green occasionally used to bridge trades or slightly balance perceived fairness. However, players only know their own valuations. That uncertainty means no one can be sure whether a counterpart likes or dislikes a color as much as they do. Players can infer some information from observed acceptances and rejections, but there are only three rounds and each player proposes three times, so there is limited time for learning. Dog and Parrot both have low acceptance thresholds and low exploitation caution, so they are likely to accept any offer that yields even small personal gains and will be quick to close workable deals. Turtle is more guarded and somewhat tougher: she will still accept good terms but is more inclined to reject marginal or confusing offers and wait for clearer gains. Numeracy and bookkeeping are moderate for all, so offers will generally respect constraints but may be somewhat simple (small numbers, clear gains) rather than highly optimized. Because multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, proposers cannot target one particular counterpart; this encourages them to craft offers that at least make sense to any likely responder. Dog and Turtle, who share valuations and both highly value blue, will see little motive to trade with each other except perhaps via small green adjustments; the most natural cross-player gains are between Parrot and either Dog or Turtle, with Parrot offloading surplus blue to get more red and Dog/Turtle doing the reverse. Over three rounds, we should see early exploratory, slightly imbalanced proposals that test whether others are willing to trade green or red, followed by one or two clearer bilateral value-creating trades where Parrot and one high-blue-value player exchange red and blue in a way that both see as clearly profitable in their private calculators. rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, all players still hold 10 of each chip, and no one has seen any trade behavior yet. Each proposer is effectively exploring: Dog and Turtle know they like blue more than red, but they do not know how others feel; Parrot knows he likes red more and blue less, but cannot tell whether others share or oppose that preference. Parrot then made a blue-for-red trade that Dog eagerly accepted, signaling to all that Dog indeed values blue relatively highly and is willing to move red to get it. Turtle’s small green-for-blue attempt was declined by both others, giving Turtle weak evidence that green is not obviously more or less valued than blue by the others, or that the terms were just not attractive enough. However, Parrot has just had a profitable Dog-facing offer declined, revealing that Dog may be reaching a point where additional red is not attractive relative to further loss of blue. Turtle has observed that Dog is a reliable trading partner for red–blue swaps, though inventory constraints and diminishing marginal gains may start to matter. With only one round left, players know that there are limited remaining opportunities. Dog and Parrot, as easy accepters, will still snap up any clear gains, but they may be somewhat more cautious about giving up too much of their now-scarcer high-value colors. All three players start with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. BLUE is low-value at $0.10 for everyone. This makes Unicorn the high-RED type who is willing to pay more for RED than the others, but nobody knows that ex ante; they only know their own values and the public ranges.

From a surplus perspective, efficient trades move chips from lower-value to higher-value holders or convert low-value colors into high-value ones for a given person. Here, BLUE is worst for everyone, GREEN is best for everyone, and RED is intermediate for Badger and Peacock but equal to GREEN for Unicorn. That means: (1) everyone likes turning BLUE into GREEN; (2) Unicorn especially likes turning BLUE or GREEN into RED if the rate is favorable; and (3) Badger and Peacock are roughly indifferent between small shifts of RED and GREEN in value terms, but still care about not giving away too much surplus. Because GREEN is common-value and obviously valuable, and BLUE is obviously weak, GREEN↔BLUE trades will be benchmarked against a natural fairness anchor of about 1 GREEN ≈ 5 BLUE (both $0.50) if they tried to keep equal value. RED is noisier because players do not know others' RED values—only that RED is somewhere in a broad range.

Persona-wise, Badger and Unicorn both have low acceptance thresholds and are relatively deal-ready, willing to accept small but positive gains rather than hold out. Peacock is more numerate and careful, with lower exchange aggressiveness and good bookkeeping, so is more likely to avoid dominated trades and to recognize when another player seems to value RED more. With only three rounds and one proposal each per round, there is limited time to learn types, but some inference can occur if someone repeatedly pushes to acquire RED or give up BLUE cheaply. Uncertainty comes from: (a) hidden private values for RED, (b) simultaneous accept decisions with random matching when both responders accept, and (c) the fact that every trade permanently changes inventories and future options.

Strategically, senders want to construct proposals that are clearly profitable for themselves (as shown in their personal surplus calculator) while still giving at least one responder a modest positive gain. Given the personas, Badger and Unicorn are willing to propose modestly self-favoring trades hoping that someone bites, whereas Peacock aims for more balanced or clearly win–win offers, especially early. Because multiple acceptances lead to random partner selection, players cannot target a specific counterpart; they may prefer terms that are acceptable to at least one responder rather than finely tailored to a single one. Over three rounds, we can expect: early exploratory, relatively mild offers; some attempts to exploit inferred RED valuations (with Unicorn trying to accumulate RED, Peacock cautiously arbitraging BLUE into GREEN/RED, Badger opportunistically taking positive gains with little search); and occasional declines when a careful player sees a worse-than-fair exchange. Overall, the likely pattern is a small number of mutually beneficial trades that move GREEN or RED toward the players who visibly care more about them, along with a few declined turns where the surplus split is too lopsided or ambiguous.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, everyone holds 10 of each color. No one has seen any proposals yet, so beliefs are anchored mainly on public ranges: GREEN clearly high, BLUE clearly low, RED in the middle. Badger, with a low acceptance threshold and limited search inclination, is inclined to try a simple, apparently fair trade that gives them a small sure gain. After Round 0: Badger has slightly fewer BLUE and one extra GREEN; Peacock has given up some RED to Unicorn in exchange for BLUE and also sold one GREEN to Badger; Unicorn has gained RED from Peacock and paid in BLUE. Badger, having secured a mild GREEN gain, remains focused on simple, positive-value opportunities and may not fully infer type differences. Peacock now has clearer evidence that Unicorn likes RED and may look for a more balanced trade, perhaps turning surplus BLUE into GREEN or RED at a margin that still yields them value. By Round 2, patterns are clearer. Unicorn has repeatedly tried to buy RED with BLUE, and both Badger and Peacock have seen these moves. A careful, numerate Peacock now has a strong posterior that Unicorn values RED at the top of the plausible range, making RED increasingly scarce and worth holding unless the BLUE payment is generous. Badger, more anchor-sensitive and low-search, mainly notices that turning BLUE into GREEN is reliably good and that RED-for-BLUE trades can be modestly profitable when Unicorn is the counterparty. Inventories now have Badger and Peacock holding fewer RED and GREEN and more BLUE than at the start, while Unicorn holds extra RED and fewer BLUE, reflecting previous accepted trades. With only three turns left in the game, players are more willing to grab any clear positive-surplus deal rather than wait. Senders design proposals that are still self-favoring but try to be within the now-learned willingness-to-pay ranges: Badger continues to seek GREEN at slightly tough rates, Peacock looks for one last balanced improvement using careful bookkeeping, and Unicorn aims for a final RED acquisition at a rate still profitable given their high RED valuation but closer to what others will accept.

Given these tendencies and the short 3-round horizon (each round with 3 proposals), it is likely that multiple trades will occur early, especially ones that move RED away from Mouse toward Dog, and some BLUE from Dog or Mouse toward Owl. This round will see Dog trying to rebalance by selling BLUE or RED for GREEN, Mouse trying again to convert unwanted RED into more valuable BLUE, and Owl being selective after a declined GREEN-for-BLUE attempt.

Going into the final round, several mutually beneficial trades have already occurred. Each player now focuses on quick, safe improvements rather than long-shot, multi-step positioning. Across three short rounds, each with a single proposal turn per player, there is limited time to discover exact valuations or implement a fully efficient allocation. Early in the game, players tentatively probe with relatively mild, slightly favorable proposals that they believe are likely to be accepted (given their low acceptance thresholds and desire to avoid rejected turns). As history accumulates, offers become more targeted: alligator tries to swap away blue for red or green from buffalo; buffalo uses green and low-valued blue to get some red from alligator or to compensate rabbit; rabbit opportunistically snaps up red from both alligator and buffalo, paying with green or blue at rates that still generate high surplus for rabbit. Overall, the most likely outcome is that rabbit ends up with a noticeable red surplus, alligator modestly increases red at buffalo’s expense while giving up some blue, and buffalo exits with more green and fewer red/blue chips, reflecting their lower private valuations and cooperative tendencies.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone still holds 10 of each chip and has only their own values in mind. No one yet knows who values which color most, so opening proposals serve both to gain surplus and to signal preferences. Given their low acceptance thresholds and good bookkeeping, alligator and rabbit are likely to propose clearly profitable, modest-size trades that they expect someone to accept. Buffalo, being less aggressive and more prosocial, will likely propose a relatively balanced-seeming trade, perhaps using their low-value blue chips to acquire more generally useful green or slightly more valuable red. In this first round, uncertainty about others’ values is highest, so offers will be conservative in size but slanted toward each sender’s best guesses: red will already start moving toward rabbit, and blue will begin shifting away from buffalo toward alligator.

By the start of round 1, trade history has already revealed some structure: alligator’s first move to acquire red using blue signals that red is relatively valuable to alligator and blue is more expendable. Buffalo’s willingness to trade away blue for green suggests blue is low-value for buffalo and green is safe and useful. Rabbit’s quick acceptance to buy red from buffalo paying green reveals that rabbit strongly values red and is willing to part with green. Everyone now has partial evidence: rabbit is a strong red buyer; buffalo is a soft seller of both blue and red; alligator is a strategic buyer of red and seller of blue. In this round, proposers can refine their targets. Alligator will try to deepen their red holdings while offloading more blue, probably pointed again at buffalo, who has shown willingness to move blue and red. Buffalo, seeing demand for red, can now strategically sell additional red to rabbit or alligator for green or blue at more favorable terms, though buffalo’s non-aggressive nature keeps offers from being too hard-nosed. Rabbit, aware that others are willing sellers of red, will push to convert green and possibly blue into red while still ensuring obvious personal gains, accepting many positive-surplus opportunities.

Entering the final round, holdings are already tilted: rabbit has accumulated extra red, alligator has traded up into more red at the cost of blue, and buffalo has likely increased their green stock while running down red and blue. Everyone now has a much clearer impression that rabbit highly values red, alligator also values red but less extremely, and buffalo is comparatively indifferent between red and green while disliking blue. With only three proposal turns left, strategic patience is limited: players with low acceptance thresholds (alligator, rabbit) are inclined to lock in any remaining positive-surplus deals rather than hold out. Alligator’s last offer is an opportunistic attempt to exchange remaining blue for green, especially targeting buffalo’s known dislike of blue, though acceptance will depend on whether buffalo feels adequately compensated. Buffalo’s final proposal will likely be small and conservative, reshuffling a bit of green versus red or blue in a way that looks obviously safe to at least one counterpart. Rabbit’s last move is a final attempt to grab any remaining underpriced red from either partner in exchange for green or blue. Because everyone can see their own surplus on screen, accepted trades in this round are those with simple, transparent benefits, and any complex or thin-margin proposals are likely to be declined given the now-clearer sense of relative valuations.
